230359.explanation: Houses are for living in, not a model for making money. The Party for the Animals wants a good distribution of housing categories in every construction project and plans for truly affordable owner-occupied homes for starters or people on a low income.

230360.explanation: A good home is a basic right. To enable young people to continue living in their own city, the Party for the Animals advocates better flow to suitable housing, more (40%) social rental homes, and a halt to the demolition and liberalization of social rental housing.

230361.explanation: Before we build new homes in Maastricht, the Party for the Animals wants to use existing buildings more efficiently. Only then will we consider densification, accompanied by extra greenery. As a last option, we build on the edge of the city. We build only where it fits the surroundings and does not come at the expense of green areas or livability.

230362.explanation: The Party for the Animals wants to expand the number of pitches for mobile homes and wants the municipality to actively cooperate in finding locations where mobile home residents, travelers, Roma, and Sinti can live according to their tradition.

230364.explanation: Investing in debt assistance is investing in public health. The Party for the Animals wants to make it possible for the municipality to take over debts under certain conditions. Ultimately, this is also cheaper for society, and the debtor can better use their energy to build a future.

230365.explanation: The Party for the Animals is against work-for-benefits requirements for those entitled to social assistance. Not only is it very undesirable that people have to work without pay, it also leads to displacement of paid work. Benefit recipients should be able to focus on talent development, and work should pay.

230366.explanation: In Maastricht, everyone should be able to participate regardless of their financial situation. The Party for the Animals wants to support people with incomes up to 130% of the social minimum with costs such as sports, culture, and veterinary care. To prevent stigmatization, all residents will receive a pass.

230368.explanation: The sale of social rental homes hinders housing flow. The Party for the Animals argues against selling social rental homes unless they are replaced one-to-one. There is a shortage of social rental housing, after all. We also pay attention to a good distribution of the share of social rental homes per neighborhood.

230369.explanation: The Party for the Animals wants fewer parking spaces in the city center. Visitors to the city center are encouraged to come by bike or public transport. However, we do support the construction of park-and-ride facilities on the city outskirts where visitors can park their cars and continue their journey by public transport.

230370.explanation: To improve livability and prevent accidents, the Party for the Animals wants a maximum speed of 30 km/h in streets without separate bike lanes. Streets without sidewalks will be transformed into (residential) home zones, with a maximum speed of 15 km/h. This also applies around schools and childcare centers.

230371.explanation: Cycling is healthy and better for the environment. The Party for the Animals therefore wants more safe cycling routes to encourage cycling as an attractive alternative to the car. New bike paths should not come at the expense of greenery or the safety of humans and animals.

230372.explanation: The Party for the Animals wants to remove parking spaces in desired locations and convert them into wider sidewalks, children's play areas, flower-rich verges, small parks, or bicycle parking facilities for a more livable city. For this, we focus on shared mobility, park-and-ride, and the use of bicycles and public transport.

230373.explanation: Train travel is a good and environmentally friendly alternative to car travel. The Party for the Animals therefore wants Maastricht to strive for a train connection with Hasselt and to work together with the province to promote international train connections.

230374.explanation: Paid parking will be introduced citywide. The Party for the Animals wants to reduce parking pressure, discourage car use, and promote other travel alternatives. Paid parking everywhere also prevents displacement to 'free' neighborhoods.

230375.explanation: Maastricht, especially the city center, has poor air quality. Fewer cars mean cleaner air and better health. Therefore, the Party for the Animals advocates a car-free city center where possible.

230376.explanation: There is a clear increase in young people with mental health problems, and the number of suicides among young people continues to rise. The Party for the Animals therefore calls for a higher budget for youth mental health care. Sufficient accessible psychological help must be available for young people.

230378.explanation: Informal caregivers are crucial for a caring society. Therefore, the Party for the Animals wants to better support informal caregivers and offer financial compensation to prevent overburdening. We advocate flexible study options for young informal caregivers and paid informal care leave.

230380.explanation: Access to (reusable) menstrual products is not always a given and can cause major discomfort. The Party for the Animals believes these products should be available in public places for those who cannot afford them, have forgotten them, or unexpectedly menstruate.

230320.explanation: A lobbying register makes politics more transparent and fairer. Citizens must be able to see with whom officials schedule appointments so it is clear who is exerting influence on the municipal government.

230321.explanation: Sometimes external experts are needed for complex dossiers, but this must be efficient, transparent, and not used too easily. Currently, such agencies are deployed too quickly.

230322.explanation: We are in principle in favor of all forms of citizen participation, including participatory budgeting. This should therefore receive more support. However, Volt believes that in the future participatory budgeting should be more representative, such as involving a randomly selected group that represents the citizens' assembly.

230323.explanation: A good alderman often naturally engages with the field of our citizens. A structural internship program for civil servants and officials can ensure this happens more regularly, enabling political Maastricht to better understand what is happening with other citizens.

230324.explanation: Volt supports (preferential) referenda under strict conditions. The preference is for deliberative democracy. Residents, guided by experts, reach binding recommendations in a citizens' council. With a citizens' council, referenda become unnecessary as those issues are dealt with there.

230325.explanation: In an uncertain world, Europe must invest in its industries. Europe is not an abstract office in Brussels-Europe is also Maastricht. Investing in Brightlands strengthens education, innovation, and the regional position. As a hub between three countries, Maastricht thus attracts talent and business activity.

230326.explanation: Volt wants to slightly increase property tax (ozb) to the level of comparable municipalities, but only if this tax on owner-occupied homes is used to build new homes. A housing-per-housing tax, in other words. This will generate 7 million euros annually to build affordable housing and purchase building land.

230327.explanation: We want to raise the tourist tax to the level of comparable tourist cities in the Netherlands. If visitors help pay for the facilities they use, the revenue can be used for sports, culture, and livability in residential neighborhoods.

230328.explanation: Volt will focus on making the city center more pedestrian- and bike-friendly. But this must not have negative consequences for entrepreneurs. On the contrary: examples from, among others, Ghent show that such a livable center is a real asset for entrepreneurs, also in residential neighborhoods.

230329.explanation: To keep our city safe and clean, the municipality must spend money: on cleaners, law enforcement, and other facilities. One way to cover this is through a dog tax.

230330.explanation: Maastricht should no longer spend money on tourism promotion. Instead, that money should go to attracting and retaining talent and business activity. Maastricht’s population is aging rapidly, which will soon become a problem for our local society.

230331.explanation: Volt supports longer opening hours for supermarkets, bakeries, and the like. Currently, we already attract many day tourists from neighboring countries. Clothing stores and other non-essential shops do not need to stay open so late on Sundays.

230332.explanation: Volt supports the current Sunday shopping policy. Non-essential shops (such as clothing stores) open only at 12:00 PM and close at 6:00 PM on Sundays. This is good because our city cannot handle more day tourists from neighboring countries, where Sunday shopping is less common.

230333.explanation: This is a fake debate: priority for status holders is legally anchored in the Netherlands. Maastricht cannot change that. Research also shows that the housing shortage is due to years of political inaction in housing construction. Abolishing priority is not possible and has hardly any effect.

230334.explanation: Too many students leave after graduating. Many say they get few opportunities to learn the language and connect with employers in the (Euro)region. With good language education and Euroregional cooperation, the municipality, UM, and Zuyd should together develop a strategy for talent retention.

230335.explanation: Language skills help all Maastricht residents participate in our border region. This is good for social cohesion and the labor market. Volt wants to offer not only free Dutch language courses to international residents but also French and German for cross-border workers, and Limburgish for other newcomers.

230336.explanation: When shelter is needed, Maastricht must contribute in solidarity and guarantee humane accommodation. Where possible, we want to shelter people more in affluent neighborhoods and less in areas with many social challenges. Europe must remain a home for people fleeing war.

230337.explanation: It is currently more attractive for people to buy a new second-hand bike than to pick one up from the depot. As a result, many bikes remain at the depot. The fine should be cost-covering for the municipality and not disproportionately high.

230338.explanation: Enforcement must be proportional, professional, and respectful. Stricter enforcement is only possible when there are concrete violations and risks. Additionally, the municipality must engage much more actively with associations to emphasize alcohol abuse prevention.

230339.explanation: Cultural free spaces strengthen creativity, encounters, and experimentation. This is part of a lively and diverse cultural city and young community.

230340.explanation: Nature-inclusive building helps against heat, water nuisance, and biodiversity loss. It makes neighborhoods more livable without taking up extra scarce space. Additionally, Volt wants Maastricht to develop a more ambitious green vision for the Euregio with its Belgian border municipalities (Lanaken and Visé).

230341.explanation: Reducing pesticides around homes protects health and nature. The municipality may go beyond the national minimum in this regard.

230342.explanation: Trees are crucial against heat, water nuisance, and poor air quality. Therefore, tree felling rules must be stricter and greening should become the norm. Additionally, Volt wants Maastricht to develop a more ambitious green vision for the Meuse-Rhine Euregio with its Belgian border municipalities (Lanaken and Visé).

230343.explanation: Volt believes there is little point today in investing more tax money to attract even more day tourists. Instead, this money can be better used to attract and retain talent and business activity.

230346.explanation: Sports facilities are important for health and social interaction. If demand increases, expansion should be explored. In our election program, we explain how we can quickly obtain the necessary finances for such sports facilities in the very short term.

230347.explanation: Volt wants to significantly increase the sports and culture budget and later double it. Sports and culture help young people preventively, sometimes better than traditional youth care. Additionally, a strong cultural policy attracts young talent. This makes Maastricht a vibrant, young, and accessible cultural city.

230348.explanation: It depends on what kind of events are meant. We are against extra Vrijthof events that attract even more day tourism. We support more neighborhood events with municipal support and more conferences that connect knowledge and talent in the border region, such as an annual Smart Meuse-Rhine conference.

230349.explanation: Swimming skills are essential for health and safety in a water-rich city, and not all families can afford lessons. Therefore, school swimming must be available.

230350.explanation: Not all children get the same support at home. Investing in additional training and homework guidance increases equal opportunities. This is even more important in a city like Maastricht, where inequality is very high.

230353.explanation: Well-trained enforcement officers ensure safety and understanding in the neighborhoods. Volt wants to invest in professional, inclusive, and accessible enforcement that acts preventively and fosters connection. Safety for women on the streets at night is a high priority for Volt.

230354.explanation: Incorrectly parked bikes obstruct pedestrians, especially people with physical disabilities. Enforcement is part of a safe, accessible cycling city but must be accompanied by more bike racks and the reduction of on-street parking spaces for tourists in the center.

230355.explanation: Camera surveillance must be proportional and purpose-driven. A safe city, for example during nightlife for women, is very important. Although cameras are not always the solution, reducing camera surveillance is undesirable.

230356.explanation: Student rooms fall under room rental, splitting, and livability tests. Municipalities may not exclude based on identity ('student'). Volt wants to focus on more housing campuses, including in Randwyck, so that the housing pressure of students can be better distributed across the entire city and not concentrated in certain neighborhoods.

230357.explanation: Maastricht currently has enough hotels. Volt wants the municipality to be more active in buying and owning land so that housing is prioritized over hotels and we do not leave scarce space to the free market.

230358.explanation: We want fewer on-street parking spaces for tourists. If a neighborhood has enough parking spaces for residents, we remove the rest. We replace them with greenery to counteract heat stress.

230359.explanation: The housing market must remain affordable for starters and middle incomes. Therefore, Volt wants the majority of new construction projects to consist of affordable rental and owner-occupied homes and social rental housing.

230360.explanation: Priority based on postal code or origin is legally almost impossible. Waiting lists for social rental housing already follow registration duration: these are mainly people with ties to Maastricht. The housing shortage is due to years of too little social housing; changing priority has little effect.

230361.explanation: Volt mainly wants to build within the city: (mid-)high-rise in modern neighborhoods like Randwyck, burying the railway, and densifying vacant areas. Expansion to the north is possible, but Volt is strongly against building behind Molenweg and other places where construction comes at the expense of nature.

230362.explanation: Trailer park residents have the right to appropriate housing. More pitches are needed to catch up and are in line with trailer residents’ rights based on European law.

230364.explanation: Debt forgiveness can help people out of hopeless situations, provided it is under strict conditions. In this way, we increase the chance of a new start without long-term poverty. We must learn from existing successful examples in Limburg and elsewhere in Europe.

230365.explanation: Participation must be encouraged through education and guidance, not through coercion or punishment. Volt wants people to participate sustainably, not to be forced to do unpaid work. Evaluations of municipalities that have conducted these experiments show limited effectiveness and negative side effects.

230366.explanation: Everyone must be able to participate in sports and culture, even with a low income. A social pass lowers barriers and promotes connection. Based on successful passes such as those from The Hague and Brussels, Volt proposes a Maastricht social pass in its program: 't Peske.

230368.explanation: The Vienna model shows how important municipal control is in combating the housing crisis. Selling social rental housing reduces the affordable stock. Volt wants to increase that instead and help starters access affordable home ownership via other routes instead of thinning out the social housing stock.

230369.explanation: Volt chooses a low-car city center with more space for greenery, pedestrians, and cyclists. Tourists park on the edge at well-connected P+R facilities, which creates more space in the city center for residents, entrepreneurs, staff, and caregivers without adding extra parking spaces.

230370.explanation: A lower speed limit makes streets safer for pedestrians and cyclists. This improves livability in residential neighborhoods. Moreover, making almost the entire city a 30 km zone makes traffic rules clearer, except where it would be unsafe (such as the Singels).

230371.explanation: Safe cycling routes connect neighborhoods and make the city more accessible, especially for children and the elderly. Cycling routes to schools and walking routes to bus stops are very important. We also want more cycling routes to Belgium. This encourages healthy mobility and reduces car traffic.

230372.explanation: Street parking takes up a lot of space. When we direct tourist traffic to the edges of the city, many parking spaces in the center can be reduced. By reducing parking spaces, space is created for greenery, widening pavements, bike racks, and meeting places.

230373.explanation: A better train connection with Hasselt strengthens the Meuse-Rhine Euregio. This is good for accessibility, the economy, and education. Therefore, it is important that the current railway bridge is renewed, a Maastricht connection with Flanders is restored, and better connections to Liège and Aachen are established.

230374.explanation: Volt wants parking policies to be tailored per neighborhood based on livability and accessibility. One residential area may find it desirable to increase parking prices for visitors, while another may not. A generic ban on expansion does not fit this approach.

230375.explanation: Volt wants the city center to direct tourist car traffic to park-and-ride facilities at the city’s edges from now on. Wherever possible, streets should be made car-free and only accessible to residents, entrepreneurs, employees, and informal caregivers.

230376.explanation: Young people are increasingly experiencing psychological pressure. Investing in mental healthcare prevents bigger problems later. This requires structurally more funding for youth care and education, but also for sports and culture that promote the (mental) health of young people.

230378.explanation: Informal caregivers play a major role in caring for the elderly and sick. Additional support prevents overload and dropout.

230380.explanation: Access to menstrual products is a basic need.

230320.explanation: In Maastricht, project developers, lobby groups, and large entrepreneurs find their way to the aldermen blindly, often behind closed doors. This leads to cronyism and undermines trust that decisions are made fairly. The SP chooses maximum transparency.

230321.explanation: The SP believes that too often expensive consultancy firms are hired, while knowledge should also be developed within the municipality. By investing in our own employees, policies become cheaper, more transparent, and better aligned with what Maastricht needs.

230322.explanation: The Citizen Budget is mere token participation: mainly articulate and well-organized residents benefit, and the application process is unnecessarily complicated. The SP therefore supports neighborhood budgets per district, where residents decide themselves about their money. They know best what their neighborhood needs.

230323.explanation: The SP thinks those who make policy should truly know the city. Therefore, the SP wants a mandatory social internship: civil servants and administrators spend working hours assisting in community centers, food banks, or healthcare, among others. This greatly reduces the gap between the city hall and the city.

230324.explanation: On truly important issues, Maastricht residents should have the final say themselves. That is why the SP wants an accessible referendum as a democratic brake. And if the result is clear, the city government may not ignore it; the plan must then go back to the drawing board.

230325.explanation: Brightlands must stand on its own feet: innovation is good, but not at the expense of livability and the opportunities for all Maastricht residents. Make the campus financially independent quickly, listen to ordinary residents, and let their voice lead towards a greener, more livable neighborhood.

230326.explanation: The property tax does not need to automatically increase. The SP believes it should only be indexed annually at most. Only if Maastricht must otherwise make difficult choices-such as closing community centers or cutting services-can a temporary extra increase be considered.

230327.explanation: Tourism generates money for a few sectors, but the costs (crowding, nuisance, and cleaning) fall on the people of Maastricht. Through a higher tourist tax, the SP lets visitors fairly contribute to livability and keeping our city clean, and we invest in the neighborhoods.

230328.explanation: This is REALLY factually impossible, so we cannot formulate a position on it, and any party that claims to do so is simply deceiving voters.

230329.explanation: The SP finds the dog tax unfair and outdated. For many people, a dog is a companion and an indispensable support in the fight against loneliness, not an unnecessary luxury to be taxed.

230330.explanation: The SP does not want to invest money in promotional pitches for companies. That money too often disappears into marketing talk and yields little for the average Maastricht resident. Invest rather in affordable housing, good jobs, strong neighborhoods, and facilities; companies will then settle on their own.

230331.explanation: The SP wants to uphold Sunday rest as much as possible. It is a day for yourself, your family, and friends, but also for things like the football club and associations that keep Maastricht running. Not every hour of the week needs to revolve around shopping and generating turnover.

230332.explanation: We want to uphold Sunday rest as much as possible. It is a day for yourself, your family, and friends, and also for the football club and associations that keep Maastricht running. Not every hour of the week needs to be about consumerism and revenue.

230333.explanation: Giving status holders no priority does not solve the housing shortage, and anyone who claims it does is lying. They are not the problem, and by doing this families end up homeless. The real problem is that for decades far too few social rental homes have been built for all of us.

230334.explanation: The SP wants a city where we live together instead of alongside each other. International students are not outsiders but people who live, work, and study here. That is why we make agreements with the university for real integration: participation in neighborhoods, clubs, and volunteer work.

230335.explanation: Free language courses for international residents are not a municipal task. The university, college, and employers already offer this to their students and employees. The municipality can better use that money for housing, care, and facilities that all Maastricht residents directly benefit from.

230336.explanation: Maastricht already hosts slightly more asylum seekers than mandatory, and that generally works very well. But even 'more than mandatory' is unfair if other municipalities sit back. We want a fair distribution and good, humane reception that everyone can reasonably sustain.

230337.explanation: Fifty euros is simply outrageous for sometimes briefly parking your bike incorrectly. For many Maastricht residents, the bicycle is not a luxury but essential to get to work, school, or care. Fifteen euros keeps a deterrent effect and allows enforcement without hitting people with limited means.

230338.explanation: Associations rely on volunteers and involved parents, not on enforcement officers and extra rules. The SP wants to support sports clubs and community centers, not saddle them with even more control and administration. Parenting is first and foremost the responsibility of parents, not the volunteers behind the bar.

230339.explanation: Cultural free spaces like Landbouwbelang are indispensable for a vibrant city. They provide space for experimentation, meeting, and counterculture. The SP has campaigned for years to protect them: no eviction without a full alternative and a permanent place for free culture.

230340.explanation: Nature-inclusive construction is not a luxury but an urgent necessity. It helps against heat stress, ensures cooler neighborhoods, and benefits health and well-being. Good housing protects people from heat and makes neighborhoods livable, especially for those without a garden or air conditioning.

230341.explanation: The health of our residents takes precedence over agricultural interests. The SP wants wider pesticide-free zones around homes, at least 250 meters. This protects people from harmful pesticides and ensures a safe, healthy living environment-especially for children and vulnerable groups.

230342.explanation: Trees are indispensable basic facilities: they keep our streets cooler, absorb water, and make the air healthier. That is why the SP wants stricter rules for tree felling and planting. Especially in paved neighborhoods, trees are pure health benefits for all of us.

230343.explanation: Maastricht does not lack tourists but livability. The SP wants to stop subsidies for the city marketing industry (like Maastricht Marketing and City Management), which mainly serve 'branding' and chains. That money should go to neighborhoods and local entrepreneurs-that’s where the real economy is.

230346.explanation: The Geusseltbad is a public basic facility. By expanding, we create more space and longer opening hours for free school swimming, recreational swimming, seniors, Wmo users, and toddlers. Swimming is healthy, safe, and for everyone, not just for those who can afford it.

230347.explanation: Youth culture is not a byproduct; it is an essential basic facility. If you have money, you can always find somewhere, but if you have tight means, you too often miss out. The SP wants every young person in Maastricht to be able to create, rehearse, and perform-in their neighborhood, affordably, and accessibly.

230348.explanation: Large events are often unaffordable for the average Maastricht resident and cause nuisance and crowding in the city. The SP prefers small-scale, accessible events and folk culture in the neighborhoods and for everyone. That is better for livability and true encounters in the city.

230349.explanation: More money is good, but the SP goes much further: we want school swimming to be completely free for all pupils in Maastricht. Swimming lessons are not a luxury or an 'extra' but a basic skill. And of course, it should not depend on your parents’ wallet.

230350.explanation: The SP wants more support for additional schooling and homework guidance, especially for children and families who need it most. Not scattergun spending, but targeted investment where the backlog is. That truly creates equal opportunities instead of staying stuck in nice words.

230353.explanation: More enforcement officers means more visibility and faster intervention in cases of nuisance. They are the eyes and ears in our neighborhoods: against illegally parked bikes, illegal waste dumping, and fraudulent rental practices. But then with much better salaries and safe, decent working conditions.

230354.explanation: Incorrectly parked bikes block sidewalks, streets, and driveways. As a result, elderly people, wheelchair users, and those with walkers or strollers often literally cannot pass. The SP therefore wants stricter inspection and enforcement so that public space is accessible to everyone again.

230355.explanation: The SP wants a safe city but not one where you are watched everywhere. Camera surveillance is a heavy measure that infringes on our privacy and is not always proven effective. The SP prefers street visibility: more enforcement officers and a neighborhood-oriented approach rather than more cameras.

230356.explanation: Students belong in Maastricht, but residential neighborhoods must remain livable. If too many houses are converted into student housing, family homes disappear and neighborhood balance is lost. We want space for both students and residents: stop growth in overloaded streets and ensure suitable housing for students.

230357.explanation: Maastricht does not have a hotel shortage but a housing shortage. Every A-location that turns into a (luxury) hotel-like the old Cinema Palace-is a missed and unacceptable opportunity for real housing. Therefore: an immediate hotel stop and use available space for affordable housing!

230358.explanation: Where possible, we want fewer parking spaces and more greenery in residential neighborhoods. Not just because it looks nice, but because trees and greenery provide cooling, reduce water nuisance, and create better play areas. We do this smartly and by neighborhood, without taking people’s cars away.

230359.explanation: Housing is a right, not a luxury. Every housing plan must include affordable owner-occupied homes up to €280,000, so people with an average income can continue to live here. No expensive developments for investors, but real homes for starters, young families, and Maastricht residents who want to build their life here.

230360.explanation: The SP believes a social rental home is for all people living, working, and participating here, not just for those born within the municipal boundaries. Connection to Maastricht is about work, family, love, informal care, or community life. Housing is a right, regardless of origin.

230361.explanation: Maastricht must build more affordable homes, and this might mean the city expands. But for the SP one thing is clear: not at the expense of our green spaces and landscape. We support expansion only where it is responsible and necessary, without harm to nature, livability, and health.

230362.explanation: Living in a mobile home is a human right. In Maastricht this has not been fulfilled for too long, and new pitches have faced unacceptable delays. The SP wants a proactive, respectful approach: acknowledge the need and speed up the provision of sufficient, suitable pitches for our travellers.

230364.explanation: Problematic debts trap people in stress and poverty. By buying off debts under generous conditions, you truly give people a fresh start. This has already been successful in other municipalities. The SP wants to break the cycle of poverty so people can move forward in life again.

230365.explanation: The SP believes in participation, but not in coercion. Not everyone on welfare can do community work due to health, stress, or home situations. Obligatory work then backfires. Those who want and can contribute should have the chance to do so, with help where needed. And based on trust, not punishment.

230366.explanation: The SP wants to introduce a Maastricht city pass, as other cities already have, so people with low incomes can participate for free or at a discount. Going to the cinema once or watching MVV is part of a happy and fulfilling life. No one should be sidelined because their wallet is empty.

230368.explanation: The social rental housing stock is already far too small. In recent years many social rental homes have been sold, resulting in extremely long waiting lists. The SP wants more good and TRULY affordable social rental homes so everyone can live affordably - not fewer by selling them off.

230369.explanation: The SP wants a truly car-reduced city center. More parking spaces attract even more cars and make the city busier and less healthy. We choose more space for greenery, pedestrians, and cyclists, with parking primarily for those who really need a car.

230370.explanation: 30 km/h zones make residential neighborhoods safer and more livable. Slower driving means fewer accidents, less noise, and more space for children to play outside and elderly to move safely. Neighborhoods are for living in, not for racing through.

230371.explanation: Safe cycling routes between neighborhoods are not a luxury, but a basic provision. For many Maastricht residents, the bicycle is the daily means of transport to work, school, and care. By investing in safe, logical routes, we make cycling attractive and safe for everyone - young and old.

230372.explanation: The SP wants fewer on-street parking spaces where possible, to create space for greenery, safe sidewalks, and pleasant streets. Not to annoy drivers, but to make our neighborhoods more livable. Car parking remains possible for those who need it, but the street belongs to everyone.

230373.explanation: Maastricht lies in the middle of the Euregio. A train connection with Hasselt makes it easier for Maastricht residents to work, visit family, and shop in Belgium. Good and affordable public transport across the border is essential, especially for those without a car.

230374.explanation: Expanding paid parking in the suburbs is necessary to keep neighborhoods livable. Now streets are full of cars with German and Belgian license plates. Residents and their visitors park for free, but those who use the neighborhood as free parking will have to pay. This keeps the street for the residents.

230375.explanation: The SP wants a truly car-reduced city center. More parking spaces attract even more cars and make the city busier and less healthy. We choose more space for greenery, pedestrians, and cyclists, with parking primarily for those who really need a car.

230376.explanation: Young people grow up in a world full of performance pressure, uncertainty, and stress. Too many get stuck and receive help too late. The SP wants more investment in (preventive) mental health care for youth: accessible and without long waiting lists. None of them should stand alone.

230378.explanation: Informal caregivers keep our care system going, often at the expense of their own time, health, and income. The SP wants more funding for their support so they can keep going: through allowances, practical help, and appreciation. Caring for each other should not be a silent exhaustion battle.

230380.explanation: Period poverty is a real problem for more and more girls and women: sanitary pads or tampons are expensive and not everyone can afford them every month. No one should be ashamed or have to resort to emergency solutions. Just free tampons and pads, like toilet paper.

230320.explanation: We as PVV believe that officials must be open and honest about who they talk to. Residents should know who is pulling the strings in our municipality.

230321.explanation: Fewer consulting firms. Municipal money should go to the Maastricht citizen. Every year large sums disappear to expensive consulting firms while the results often end up forgotten in a drawer.

230323.explanation: Officials and civil servants must know what is really going on in society. However, this must not result in additional costs.

230324.explanation: The city belongs to its residents, not to officials, civil servants, or activist groups. Too often far-reaching decisions are made over the heads of Maastricht residents. A referendum ensures the municipal government must listen to what the majority of residents truly want.

230325.explanation: We believe no extra taxpayer money should go to large, expensive projects like Brightlands. First, the basics for residents must be in order: affordable housing, safe neighborhoods, good elderly care, and less bureaucracy and waste.

230326.explanation: The property tax (ozb) should actually be reduced. Maastricht residents already face rising costs for housing, energy, groceries, and care. A higher property tax means yet more burden for hard-working citizens, families, and the elderly.

230327.explanation: Pressure on the city is increasing due to mass tourism. It is therefore reasonable that visitors should pay more. At the same time, an increase cannot harm local entrepreneurs. The revenues must visibly flow back to the city and its residents.

230328.explanation: These (Maastricht) entrepreneurs should not be burdened with extra municipal regulations. This piling up of rules leads to higher costs and less freedom to do business.

230329.explanation: More and more municipalities are abolishing the dog tax. As the capital, we should set a good example too.

230330.explanation: We must first focus on entrepreneurs in Maastricht itself.

230331.explanation: People have increasingly busy weeks. Being able to shop on Sunday evening gives residents more freedom to organize their own time.

230332.explanation: What was once intended as extra freedom has in practice become an obligation. Let the entrepreneurs decide themselves if they want to open on Sundays.

230333.explanation: Our city is FULL. Maastricht residents have been on waiting lists for years. Therefore, Maastricht residents should be prioritized and NOT status holders.

230334.explanation: The primary responsibility lies with the university itself. It cannot be that municipal resources - which are desperately needed for housing, safety, care, and livability - are used to solve problems caused by internationalization.

230335.explanation: Offering free language courses means that the Maastricht taxpayer pays for a responsibility that lies with the individual. Those who choose to live in Maastricht must also make the effort to speak Dutch themselves.

230336.explanation: Our city is full. No more growth. Housing is scarce, facilities are under pressure, and the streets are becoming increasingly unsafe. There is no more space.

230337.explanation: We support a reduction so residents are not unnecessarily burdened and the threshold to collect a bike is lower.

230338.explanation: Associations are the backbone of our Maastricht community. They run largely on volunteers and are already overloaded with rules and inspections. There must be trust in our associations as long as they responsibly handle alcohol and protect youth. The municipality should support, not punish.

230339.explanation: The municipality must choose safety and livability, not subsidizing or protecting free spaces. Culture is fine, but without exceptions: the same laws and regulations apply everywhere and everyone must follow the same rules.

230340.explanation: This increases costs and complicates construction. We want faster and affordable building without extra rules so that Maastricht residents get a house sooner.

230341.explanation: We oppose expanding pesticide-free zones beyond national regulations. Extra local requirements make agriculture and green management unnecessarily harder and more expensive. National standards are sufficient.

230342.explanation: PVV Maastricht opposes even stricter rules for tree cutting. This delays projects and makes maintenance unnecessarily complicated. Current rules already provide sufficient protection.

230343.explanation: Maastricht is already a busy tourist city. Additional promotion costs a lot of money while residents increasingly experience nuisance from crowds, housing shortages, and rising costs in the city center. The municipality should invest more in amenities for residents.

230346.explanation: The Geusseltbad is important for swimming lessons, sports, and health. By investing in the Geusseltbad, it can better meet the growing demand from Maastricht residents.

230347.explanation: The municipality already spends a lot on culture. Additional investments are not necessary. Priority should be given to basic facilities for young people such as meeting places and sports.

230348.explanation: We want to focus more on existing Maastricht festivals, local traditions, and initiatives from existing entrepreneurs in Maastricht.

230349.explanation: Water safety is essential. Not every child has the opportunity to take swimming lessons at home. By supporting school swimming, the municipality ensures that all children learn to swim.

230350.explanation: PVV Maastricht supports extra investment in further training and homework guidance. Young people deserve equal opportunities and good support. This strengthens education and future prospects for families in Maastricht.

230353.explanation: Nuisance, pollution, and insecurity are increasing. Additional public order officers ensure visibility, rapid action, and a safer Maastricht for residents.

230354.explanation: The municipality should indeed take stricter action against incorrectly parked bicycles. They cause a lot of nuisance. Stricter enforcement keeps Maastricht tidy and accessible.

230355.explanation: There should actually be more camera surveillance.

230356.explanation: The pressure and nuisance in residential neighborhoods have increased significantly. Residents, especially the elderly and families, see livability and safety decline due to the growth of student housing. Students are welcome, but not at the expense of our residents; they deserve priority for a safe and livable neighborhood.

230357.explanation: The city is already saturated and new hotels cause additional pressure and nuisance.

230358.explanation: PVV Maastricht is against removing parking spaces in residential neighborhoods. Residents must be able to park their cars close by.

230359.explanation: By including affordable homes up to €280,000 in every construction project, starters and families get more opportunities and the housing market becomes more accessible.

230360.explanation: The Maastricht resident who has been on the waiting list for years should always be given priority.

230361.explanation: Yes, only where it is really necessary and responsible – always putting the Maastricht resident first.

230362.explanation: We must think more along with caravan site residents. PVV Maastricht wants to stand up for Maastricht residents who have lived here for a long time. Helping our own residents first.

230364.explanation: Residents with heavy debts should be able to get a new chance. Debt forgiveness can help to move forward again but only under strict conditions. This way support remains fair and targeted at Maastricht residents who really need it.

230365.explanation: PVV Maastricht believes that those receiving social assistance benefits should, where possible, provide a social contribution. This provides structure, contributes to society, and keeps social assistance fair for everyone who really needs it.

230366.explanation: PVV supports a social pass for residents with a low income. This allows them to participate for free or at a discount. This strengthens health and participation in Maastricht without high costs for residents.

230368.explanation: PVV Maastricht believes that social tenants should be given the opportunity to buy their home where possible. This increases ownership and turnover. The municipality may encourage housing corporations in this as long as it remains affordable for residents of Maastricht.

230369.explanation: The city center has too little (affordable!) parking capacity. This hinders accessibility for residents and visitors.

230370.explanation: Many streets are already safe enough and additional zones cause even more frustration and unnecessary delays. We choose targeted enforcement in real problem streets.

230371.explanation: PVV supports more investment in safe cycling routes between neighborhoods. Good lighting, visibility, and safe crossings increase traffic safety for residents.

230372.explanation: Reducing parking spaces causes more parking nuisance in surrounding neighborhoods. Accessibility must come first: sufficient parking facilities.

230373.explanation: We are against costs being borne by Maastricht. First invest in safety, livability, and accessibility within our own city.

230374.explanation: It unnecessarily impacts residents financially and solves little. We choose affordable and accessible parking for residents.

230375.explanation: This limits accessibility for residents, the elderly, and entrepreneurs.

230376.explanation: PVV Maastricht supports extra investment in youth mental health care. Waiting times are far too long and problems are piling up. Fast and accessible help is essential for a healthy future for young people in Maastricht.

230378.explanation: Informal caregivers are indispensable to our society. The municipality must better appreciate and support these people. With extra resources, we can provide more respite care, less bureaucracy, and real appreciation for their efforts.

230380.explanation: PVV Maastricht believes the municipality should not take on extra tasks and costs that do not belong to its core duties. Free menstrual products lead to structural costs and extra rules; for people with financial problems, targeted schemes already exist where better investment can be made.

230320.explanation: Given recent experiences, we notice this is necessary. Transparency is absolutely essential!

230321.explanation: The council and executive need to look at themselves. More investment in the own organization and if no other option, preferably with local entrepreneurs. Ensuring that contracts with external parties do not just confirm what the client wants.

230322.explanation: More money may be available to residents to decide on their own priorities, but the current method of participatory budgeting does not seem to be the best approach.

230323.explanation: No obligation, but if it fits in the personal development plan, an internship is recommended.

230324.explanation: The referendum should then be binding according to the Swiss model. The choice must be clearly defined.

230325.explanation: Decisions have been made to develop Brightlands and no additional funds are needed.

230326.explanation: An increase in property tax also affects residents already struggling to make ends meet. Financial challenges should primarily be tackled by limiting expenditures.

230327.explanation: Maastricht is an overheated tourist city. A financial incentive can slow growth and ensure it becomes manageable.

230328.explanation: In specific local situations, action must be possible, taking into account that Maastricht should not price itself out of the market. That is why you are a local administrator.

230329.explanation: 50PLUS agrees that the costs of dog ownership for the municipality should be funded by the dog tax.

230330.explanation: Invest more money in making Maastricht an attractive location by offering good facilities, rather than doing even more promotion.

230331.explanation: 50PLUS has always been against the expansion. This will certainly come at the expense of community life and the small entrepreneurs who are then forced to participate.

230332.explanation: 50PLUS has always been against the expansion. This will certainly come at the expense of community life and the small entrepreneurs who are then forced to participate.

230333.explanation: 50PLUS believes that this is discrimination against the local population. Solutions are needed for the housing problem, but this will not be solved by disadvantaging the local residents.

230334.explanation: The responsibility for integration should lie with the university itself. The programs should primarily be given in Dutch, which will lead to fewer students.

230335.explanation: There are no free courses. The primary responsibility lies with the asylum seeker centers (AZCs), educational institutions, or other organizations.

230336.explanation: 50PLUS considers the legal requirements sufficient. The asylum reception center of Eijsden-Margraten was facilitated just outside the city limits of Maastricht, and the residents of Maastricht also have to deal with it.

230337.explanation: 50PLUS observes that improperly parked bicycles endanger the safety of the elderly and residents with disabilities. It is simply common decency to park your bike in the right place. It is a major problem that needs to be addressed.

230338.explanation: The rules for hospitality venues and associations should be enforced in the same way, so there is no need to make them stricter.

230339.explanation: 50PLUS does not see added value in this.

230340.explanation: It is better, cheaper, and easier to incorporate this immediately during new construction. In the long term, nature must be protected if we want to pass it on well to future generations.

230341.explanation: 50PLUS considers the residents’ interest in avoiding health damage important here.

230342.explanation: Nature must be protected. In case of disease or danger, trees should be cut, but not removed too easily when developing plans.

230343.explanation: An economy that relies too much on tourism is vulnerable. Maastricht must ensure that its economy is not solely based on tourism and the university and should therefore become more diverse.

230346.explanation: It would be better if a swimming pool were located on the west side. That way it cannot happen that during major maintenance swimming is impossible in the entire city.

230347.explanation: There is more than enough offer for young people.

230348.explanation: There are sufficient large events in Maastricht. The MECC itself is responsible for organizing and attracting their events.

230349.explanation: A good idea, but in practice school swimming encounters many practical obstacles. However, parents should be encouraged to ensure their children are self-reliant in the water.

230350.explanation: This is a primary responsibility of educational institutions and parents. Where necessary, the institution should provide and facilitate opportunities for those who need extra attention.

230353.explanation: 50PLUS observes that safety is still a problem in Maastricht. Enforcement remains a high priority.

230354.explanation: Check and take effective measures by removing bicycles. These improperly parked bicycles endanger the safety of elderly and disabled residents.

230355.explanation: In this era, technological possibilities must be used to improve the city. The use of smart cameras, which are usable for multiple purposes, is recommended.

230356.explanation: The rhythm of life of students and other residents generally does not align. Therefore, further mixing between these groups is not desirable.

230357.explanation: More than enough hotels exist, and several are still under construction. We must beware of overkill.

230358.explanation: Parking spaces must be available for residents so that they can park their car within a reasonable distance from their home. If there is a surplus of parking spaces in a specific neighborhood, the preferred alternative is to green the area.

230359.explanation: 50PLUS prefers lifetime-proof homes that can promote flow-through to help partly solve the housing problem.

230360.explanation: 50PLUS fully understands that local residents want to live in the city and wants to give them the possibility to realize this.

230361.explanation: Not needed and also seems difficult to realize. There are still sufficient possibilities to expand vertically. It is necessary to carefully assess which homes will be needed in the future.

230362.explanation: It has been established that there is a challenge here and that it is not easy to find a good location for houseboats within our city.

230364.explanation: 50PLUS wants to combat the profit model of collection agencies that earn money from debt problems to give residents a new chance, but there must be clear guarantees that the resident does not relapse.

230365.explanation: 50PLUS does believe that this must suit the individual. There are enough social challenges where necessary help is lacking, but it must not lead to displacement of jobs.

230366.explanation: 50PLUS does not want residents to be sidelined for financial reasons. However, care must be taken to ensure that people do not want to participate less in the labor process because they lose facilities.

230368.explanation: But caution is needed here to prevent speculation and that these homes are sold in the short term.

230369.explanation: 50PLUS advocates for a car-reduced city center with Park and Ride facilities at the edges of the city with good connections.

230370.explanation: The time gain for motorists or other road users is minimal, and speed reduction greatly increases safety in neighborhoods. This also offers children better opportunities to play outside.

230371.explanation: Good cycling routes are a boost for biking, an important priority within the STOP principle. In this way, residents are encouraged to leave the car behind and use the bike. Care must be taken to accommodate different users of cycling routes (electric bikes).

230372.explanation: Residents must always have the opportunity to park their car near their home.

230373.explanation: There is currently a train from Maastricht to Visé-Liège-Tongeren-Hasselt. That is absurd. Instead of legal disputes, the connection between the two capitals of Limburg and two universities must be improved, as well as the connection to Flanders. Preserving the railway bridge is essential in this.

230374.explanation: Residents of Maastricht do not want tourists to park in their streets. Maastricht does not invest in Park & Ride lots, which unfortunately causes this to happen. As a result, family members/friends have to pay the price.

230375.explanation: This benefits the city as well as pedestrians and cyclists. It might be worth considering whether options such as fatbikes should also be restricted in these streets.

230376.explanation: 50PLUS believes that the solution is not in spending more money. Necessary care must be deliverable within financial constraints. But it has also been established that care providers have not conducted responsible financial management.

230378.explanation: The increasing aging population means more informal caregivers are needed. The practical limitations they encounter must be resolved as much as possible.

230380.explanation: 50PLUS wants residents to be well informed about where to turn. The number of places must be sufficient, and educational institutions also have an important role in this.

230320.explanation: The Socially Active Citizens' Party (SAB) supports transparency in governance but opposes unnecessary bureaucracy. A lobby register can be valuable, provided it is practically designed and truly contributes to insight for residents.

230321.explanation: SAB believes policy should be developed as much as possible using internal knowledge. External consultancy firms are expensive, result in knowledge loss, and reduce the council's control. External expertise is only appropriate for specialized or temporary issues.

230322.explanation: The Socially Active Citizens' Party values citizen participation but does not see participatory budgeting as an end goal. First, it must be shown that the current budget reaches all neighborhoods and delivers real results. More funding only makes sense if it is demonstrably effective.

230323.explanation: SAB believes policy improves when administrators and civil servants know the practice. Regularly working alongside neighborhoods and organizations increases understanding, improves decisions, and aligns with SAB's focus on people-oriented and practical governance.

230324.explanation: SAB values input from residents but does not see referenda as a standard instrument. For complex issues, SAB opts for good participation, neighborhood discussions, and clear decision-making by the council so that interests...

230325.explanation: SAB recognizes the importance of Brightlands Maastricht Health Campus but believes extra municipal funding is only justified with demonstrable added value for employment, healthcare, and residents. First results and transparency, then additional resources.

230326.explanation: SAB believes that property tax has already risen sharply in recent years. At a time when many residents are under financial pressure, further increases are undesirable. SAB chooses cost control and critical evaluation of expenses rather than higher charges. The BSGW tax should decrease if the municipality has surplus funds.

230327.explanation: SAB believes it is reasonable that visitors contribute more to the costs of congestion, management, and facilities in Maastricht. This way, residents are spared, and the city can remain livable and accessible without higher charges for Maastricht residents.

230328.explanation: SAB believes entrepreneurs need room to operate. Additional local regulations on top of national legislation create unnecessary burdens and uncertainty. The municipality should be clear, predictable, and practical, not obstructive.

230329.explanation: SAB finds the dog tax unfair and outdated. Dog owners already pay for care and facilities, while the revenue does not directly benefit dog-related policies. Abolishing the tax is fairer and fits with lower local charges.

230330.explanation: SAB believes Maastricht becomes attractive through good accessibility, affordable space, and clear rules-not through expensive promotional campaigns. First invest in the basic conditions for entrepreneurs; promotion will follow naturally once the foundation is sound.

230331.explanation: SAB believes Sundays and public holidays should offer space for rest and family. Mandatory extensions of opening hours put pressure on staff and small businesses. Freedom of choice for entrepreneurs is fine, but no forced further extensions.

230332.explanation: SAB recognizes that opinions about Sunday shopping vary among residents and entrepreneurs. SAB chooses tailored approaches, local consideration, and citizen input. However, we are not strictly opposed to fewer Sunday openings if entrepreneurs and residents accept this.

230333.explanation: SAB believes social rental homes should be fairly allocated based on waiting time and urgency. Priority for recognized refugees is not fair to other housing seekers. Nonetheless, humane, temporary shelter remains necessary.

230334.explanation: SAB believes international students should become better integrated into the city. Strengthening cooperation with Maastricht University improves integration, livability, and mutual understanding between students and residents.

230335.explanation: SAB believes language is key to participation. Free language courses help international residents integrate faster, find work, and connect in Maastricht. This strengthens social cohesion and self-reliance.

230336.explanation: SAB believes Maastricht must meet national obligations but not exceed them. Additional shelter strains housing and facilities. SAB nevertheless supports humane, temporary shelter within clear and feasible boundaries.

230337.explanation: SAB finds the €50 fine excessive and disproportionate. A lower fee of €15 is fairer, prevents bicycles from being abandoned, and keeps enforcement proportional. The measure should correct, not punish.

230338.explanation: SAB believes existing alcohol regulations should be carefully and consistently enforced, especially to protect young people. Extra strict enforcement or new inspections are unnecessary; focus on clear rules, prevention, and cooperation with associations.

230339.explanation: SAB has no explicit position on free spaces but supports room for local initiatives, grassroots culture, and social interaction. Cultural free spaces fit this vision, provided they are safe, feasible, and balanced with the city.

230340.explanation: SAB believes new construction must be future-proof. Nature-inclusive building, such as nest boxes and greenery, enhances biodiversity and quality of life. This fits practical and responsible building, provided it remains affordable and feasible.

230341.explanation: SAB believes health must be protected within national regulations. Additional local bans on top of national standards lead to fragmentation and uncertainty. First comply with and enforce existing laws, rather than constantly adding stricter local rules.

230342.explanation: SAB believes trees must be well protected but is cautious about ever stricter rules. Enforce existing rules carefully, ensure replanting, and weigh safety, livability, and building needs practically and balanced.

230343.explanation: SAB believes Maastricht does not need additional tourist pressure. Less money for promotion and more focus on livability, spreading visitors, and quality spares residents and entrepreneurs. The city attracts visitors even without expensive campaigns.

230346.explanation: SAB recognizes the importance of the Geusseltbad swimming pool but considers expansion only justified with demonstrable necessity, affordability, and clear added value for residents. First use and optimize existing facilities, then consider expansion.

230347.explanation: SAB considers culture important for youth development and social interaction. More attention to affordable and accessible cultural offerings strengthens talent, social cohesion, and prospects. Use resources purposefully, close to youth and with visible results.

230348.explanation: SAB believes events must match the city's capacity. Not more events in number, but the right events that balance livability, safety, and costs. Quality and distribution are more important than growth for growth's sake.

230349.explanation: SAB believes school swimming is important for safety and equal opportunities. Not every child learns to swim at home. Municipal support prevents drowning risks and ensures all children can participate safely and fully.

230350.explanation: SAB finds retraining and homework guidance essential for equal opportunities. Targeted support helps students and job seekers progress, prevents setbacks, and strengthens self-reliance. Investing in people yields lasting social returns.

230353.explanation: Politics in The Hague gives too little room for more blue presence on the street, so SAB wants to allocate more funds to recruit, properly train, and grant adequate powers to Special Enforcement Officers (BOAs).

230354.explanation: Incorrectly parked bicycles have long been an eyesore for Maastricht residents and must be strictly enforced. However, at the same time, sufficient and affordable bicycle parking facilities must be realized.

230355.explanation: SAB wants more and faster deployment of camera surveillance in places with frequent nuisance and extra use of mobile cameras, also in the neighborhoods.

230356.explanation: SAB believes residential neighborhoods must primarily remain livable for residents. Unrestrained growth of student housing leads to nuisance and displacement. Choose balance: concentrate student housing in suitable locations, not in every residential area.

230357.explanation: SAB believes new hotels are only desirable if they demonstrably fit Maastricht's carrying capacity and livability. No general ban, but tailored approaches: quality, location, and added value to the city are decisive.

230358.explanation: SAB values greening but opposes a generic reduction of parking spaces in residential areas. Parking pressure differs per neighborhood. Choose tailored solutions: add greenery where possible, without unnecessarily disadvantaging residents.

230359.explanation: SAB wants to promote affordable home ownership but opposes a mandatory quota in every building plan. The housing market varies by location. Choose tailored approaches, mixes of housing types, and affordability through smart agreements, not rigid rules.

230360.explanation: SAB finds it reasonable that residents of Maastricht who live and wait here receive as much priority as possible for social rental housing. This promotes fairness and support, within national rules and with attention to urgency.

230361.explanation: SAB believes housing construction is necessary, but expansion of Maastricht is not a given. First focus on infill, reuse, and densification where possible. Expansion only if it is careful, feasible, and landscape-responsible.

230362.explanation: SAB believes that caravan residents also have the right to adequate housing. Additional pitches are needed to address disadvantages and ensure equal treatment, within clear agreements on location, livability, and management.

230364.explanation: SAB believes that buying off problematic debts can help give people perspective under strict conditions. It prevents long-term poverty and higher social costs, provided it is combined with guidance, responsibility, and clear agreements.

230365.explanation: SAB opposes a general work obligation for social assistance recipients. Those not yet in a reintegration program can be encouraged to engage in voluntary, meaningful activities. Customization, guidance, and perspective come first, not coercion.

230366.explanation: SAB believes participation should not depend on income. A social pass makes sports, culture, and courses accessible to people with low incomes and strengthens health, development, and social cohesion. However, the pass must not be stigmatizing, so it should rather be a kind of discount pass up to 100%.

230368.explanation: SAB believes social tenants who want and are able should have the opportunity to buy their home. This increases housing security and ownership. However, the social rental stock must be maintained and sales carried out responsibly.

230369.explanation: SAB believes parking in Maastricht city center must be balanced with livability and accessibility. The principle is not more or less, but tailored per area, with good alternatives and clear choices.

230370.explanation: SAB considers traffic safety important and wants more 30 km/h zones in residential neighborhoods. The situation varies per neighborhood. Choose customization: 30 km/h where it genuinely improves safety.

230371.explanation: SAB believes safe cycling routes between neighborhoods are essential for traffic safety, health, and accessibility. Targeted investments reduce accidents, encourage cycling, and make daily travel safer and more attractive for young and old alike.

230372.explanation: SAB opposes a generic reduction of street parking. Parking pressure varies by neighborhood. Choose customization: improve where possible, maintain where needed, always with attention to livability and accessibility in Maastricht.

230373.explanation: Important because of the connection between the universities of Maastricht and Hasselt.

230374.explanation: SAB believes paid parking in the outer neighborhoods puts pressure on the livability and accessibility for residents. Parking problems differ per neighborhood and require tailored solutions, not further expansion of paid parking as a standard solution.

230375.explanation: SAB is not automatically in favor of car-free streets. No generic expansion, but tailor-made per street with attention to accessibility for residents, entrepreneurs, and emergency services in Maastricht.

230376.explanation: SAB considers the mental health of young people crucial. Extra efforts on prevention, early detection, and low-threshold help prevent more serious problems later and strengthen opportunities, wellbeing, and safety for young people in Maastricht.

230378.explanation: SAB believes informal care is indispensable. Additional support prevents overburdening, keeps care affordable, and strengthens self-reliance. Investing in informal caregivers is people-centered, prevents heavier care needs, and fits a social and practical policy.

230380.explanation: SAB believes menstrual products are basic necessities. Free provision prevents poverty and exclusion, especially among young people and those with low incomes, and contributes to health, dignity, and equal opportunities.

230320.explanation: Transparency is an important value for good governance.

230321.explanation: Costs for external consultancy agencies are rising rapidly. The municipality can spend its money more efficiently. M:OED wants to keep knowledge as much as possible in-house.

230322.explanation: M:OED advocates for more direct democracy to involve residents; the participatory budget does not contribute because it often reaches only a small group and does not help disadvantaged groups. Extra funds are better used for democratic renewal such as a student council.

230323.explanation: Contact with society is good, but this is not a workable solution.

230324.explanation: Asking for an opinion is good. A referendum is not the solution.

230325.explanation: This money can be used for young people to start a business, or for social and student housing in a lively area.

230326.explanation: This leads, among others, to higher housing costs for tenants, which are already high enough.

230327.explanation: The city must invest to make the city more sustainable. M:OED believes tourists should also contribute to this.

230328.explanation: Extra rules give the municipality more room to take the lead in areas such as sustainability.

230329.explanation: Dog tax is an important source of income for the municipality.

230330.explanation: A large and diverse job offer is needed for young people to keep them in Maastricht.

230331.explanation: It is incomprehensible that supermarkets close already at 6 p.m. Longer opening hours can promote a healthier and more sustainable lifestyle. M:OED has always advocated this.

230332.explanation: Sunday shopping offers working people the chance to visit the shops.

230333.explanation: It must be ensured that everyone has a fair chance to get a home. But if needs are very high, some asylum seekers should be able to get priority.

230334.explanation: (International) students are an important group that receives too little attention.

230335.explanation: It is good if everyone who wants to learn Dutch can do so, especially if this improves employment.

230336.explanation: Every city must do its part. Maastricht does not need to make extra efforts for this.

230337.explanation: Maastricht is excessively expensive compared to other cities. It is now unnecessarily difficult to pick up your bike, while a bike is an essential means of transport. M:OED wants a bike-friendly Maastricht.

230338.explanation: Associations are important places of connection. They should be supported, not made things harder for.

230339.explanation: M:OED believes there should be more (alternative) culture for young people. De Landbouwbelang is a good example of this.

230340.explanation: Nature-inclusive building does not have to be more expensive. It provides health benefits and a proven increase in living comfort. Greening is the future; it also helps against heat stress.

230341.explanation: There is clear evidence that pesticides are harmful to public health.

230342.explanation: A green city is a pleasant city to live in.

230343.explanation: Tourists find the city well. Extra money to attract them is not necessary.

230346.explanation: Especially outdoor facilities should be more fun and diverse, because swimming is very healthy.

230347.explanation: M:OED believes there is too little culture for the younger age group. Young people must be facilitated to organize things themselves.

230348.explanation: Yes, but M:OED believes this should involve events for young people, such as a festival.

230349.explanation: Everyone should be able to swim, regardless of their parents' income. It also contributes to the health of Maastricht residents.

230350.explanation: Guidance contributes to equal opportunities and should not only be available to wealthier residents.

230353.explanation: The only area that requires additional manpower is extra security in nightlife.

230354.explanation: The municipality must provide more bicycle parking facilities. M:OED does not want to punish cyclists for the lack of these.

230355.explanation: As long as the balance between privacy and safety is monitored, there is no need to reduce camera surveillance.

230356.explanation: More student housing must be built in the city center. The municipality should not only construct dull studio complexes. There is too much regulation that complicates addressing the housing problem.

230357.explanation: The municipality should be very cautious about allowing new hotels. However, if there are hotels with innovative concepts, they should have the opportunity to come to Maastricht.

230358.explanation: A greener city is pleasant to live in. Some parking spaces can be used for bicycle parking.

230359.explanation: It is important that young people can access affordable housing.

230360.explanation: If you want to bring the knowledge economy to the city, people from outside the city must have an equal chance at housing.

230361.explanation: It is important that there are sufficient affordable homes for young people.

230362.explanation: M:OED considers good housing important for every culture but prefers regular homes over additional camping sites. We focus on structural housing development and mixed living arrangements to promote integration and to stimulate the housing market for students and starters.

230364.explanation: It is the only way some people can get out of their problems. This prevents future problems and helps people quickly become full participants in society again.

230365.explanation: It is good if everyone who is able contributes to society in some way, also through volunteer work.

230366.explanation: M:OED believes it should be available to everyone. It is important that students and others with a low income can use it. This is an opportunity to integrate students into Maastricht life.

230368.explanation: This causes a reduction in the supply of social rental homes. Housing corporations should build more for students and young people instead.

230369.explanation: Maastricht must move towards a low-car city center with fewer car parking spaces and more space for cyclists, pedestrians, and greenery.

230370.explanation: This contributes to safety and makes the street more pleasant.

230371.explanation: A good cycling network must be developed both inside and outside the center. M:OED has always advocated for this.

230372.explanation: This should be converted into greenery and bicycle parking. There must also be P+R locations on the edge of the city.

230373.explanation: It is important that Maastricht gets good train connections and can serve as the gateway to Europe. A train connection to Hasselt improves access to cities like Brussels and Antwerp.

230374.explanation: It often costs more than it yields in the suburbs. P+R locations must also be established on the outskirts of the city.

230375.explanation: Completely agree, so there is more space for cyclists, pedestrians, and greenery. P+R locations must also be established on the outskirts of the city.

230376.explanation: Youth mental health care is facing major problems, such as long waiting times. A sustainable solution must be found to tackle this issue.

230378.explanation: Informal caregivers play an important role, also in reducing pressure on healthcare.

230380.explanation: Period poverty is a very serious issue. Menstruation should never be a reason to stay home. Providing these products for free is essential for equal opportunities.

230321.explanation: Civil servants must be knowledgeable enough to make judgments and take responsibility instead of shirking duties and wasting millions on consultancy firms.

230322.explanation: Since a citizen budget usually does not concern major issues, the influence is limited. It is more about giving groups the idea that they have influence. It involves small neighborhood matters, for example, a bench or a water fountain.

230323.explanation: Policy and execution are disconnected at the municipality. Policymakers do not sufficiently see how policy works out in practice, while implementers have little influence. Therefore, they should not only do internships but also discuss policy together, otherwise an internship has little value.

230324.explanation: Give citizens a voice, especially on important issues.

230325.explanation: Citizens probably do not know that millions are already invested in Brightlands. If there is extra money anywhere, Brightlands usually wins prizes again.

230326.explanation: No, this is already very high in Maastricht.

230327.explanation: The last increase was quite severe.

230328.explanation: Sometimes rules are needed to prevent undesirable things. For example, blocking sidewalks with delivery scooters will require an additional regulation.

230329.explanation: Most municipalities have already abolished the dog tax. Maastricht must follow now.

230330.explanation: The economic department must stop spending money on advertisements and promotions; they should finally actively recruit companies here that provide permanent well-paid jobs matching the skills of the unemployed. So, get to work actively.

230331.explanation: Staff also need days off. There are plenty of opportunities to shop.

230332.explanation: Leave it to the entrepreneurs. If they don't earn anything on a Sunday, they will close themselves.

230333.explanation: Mass immigration must stop and the Distribution Act only facilitates this. We already have a shortage of housing, space, and nature for our own population, so taking in more asylum seekers and status holders is a bad idea. We oppose preferential treatment for this group.

230334.explanation: The university actively recruits students from abroad at the expense of Dutch students. It should bear not only the benefits but also the burdens and responsibilities. It shifts these onto the population, municipality, and police. The municipality has been encouraging the university for years, but to no avail.

230336.explanation: We must not facilitate mass immigration.

230337.explanation: A bike parking facility costing millions has been built at the station, but bikes are still parked on sidewalks and squares. That is selfish. Therefore, the 50-euro fine must remain, also for students. Sometimes a financial incentive is needed to change behavior.

230338.explanation: For example, when it comes to alcohol abuse with serious consequences due to alcohol poisoning and vandalism at student associations.

230339.explanation: It's good if an alternative is provided.

230340.explanation: There should also be lawns and small parks created.
