230341.explanation: Health takes priority, and dispersal agents also end up in the groundwater.

230343.explanation: The economics department thinks mass is cash. That must change. The livability of the city is decreasing. Mass tourism makes the city unattractive.

230346.explanation: There are waiting lists for swimming lessons, disabled people can't attend due to overcrowding, and people are exercising more and more.

230347.explanation: There should be a music school again for young people that also offers progression to the conservatory. Lessons at Kumulus in painting, theater, etc., could become more affordable.

230348.explanation: The event calendar is packed, and we must not completely impair the livability of the city. There is already too much going on every weekend.

230349.explanation: Schools will then be obliged to facilitate school swimming as well. So far, few schools want to organize school swimming because they find it too much work.

230350.explanation: That is a task for the school. The school should do that. The municipality has no authority.

230353.explanation: The Liberal Party Maastricht has been asking for this for years, but the council does not cooperate. Enforcement is apparently not a priority. Wages must increase, which is being refused. Many enforcers move to other municipalities due to poor pay, high workload caused by high absenteeism.

230354.explanation: The municipality knows where bike 'salami slicing' occurs, but enforcement is instructed to take little action. Soft measures like charm offensives don't work. Only immediate cleanup helps. The problem can be solved, but enforcement is being scaled down and lacks priority.

230355.explanation: To catch offenders and increase safety, more cameras must be installed.

230356.explanation: Student campuses should be built, then family homes will become available. Overmaze is a good location for a student campus.

230357.explanation: We need housing; there are enough hotels.

230358.explanation: Both are indispensable. People simply need a car.

230359.explanation: The Liberal Party Maastricht introduced and passed the motion 'more affordable housing' in 2020 for the average Maastricht resident without a hefty salary but with a regular income. They should also be able to buy a home here. We represent the interests of the people, not the investor's wallet.

230360.explanation: The local population must always have priority over status holders.

230361.explanation: An analysis of the real needs taking into account decreasing aging and low growth of the native population. Mass immigration must stop; it puts enormous pressure on our country. There is a shortage of space, housing, and accommodation. More than 1,000 homes have already been added.

230364.explanation: That would cost millions. Better to ensure that people don't get involved with shady companies and poor guardians, plus a coach for how to manage money.

230365.explanation: If there is a labor shortage, we support paid jobs instead of volunteer work. If that is unfeasible, then volunteer work.

230366.explanation: What this social pass wants to arrange is already possible. There are schemes people have been able to rely on for years that cover exactly these matters.

230368.explanation: The stock must be maintained at an adequate level.

230369.explanation: Because a lot is being built in the city center.

230371.explanation: But also rules are needed for fatbikes and electric bikes that drive very fast. They are usually a danger to pedestrians and other road users.

230372.explanation: If more homes are added, parking spaces are needed.

230374.explanation: Only expand when residents themselves request it.

230375.explanation: People also live in the city center; does that mean they may no longer drive home? May emergency services no longer come? What about people with limited mobility, are they no longer allowed to be dropped off in those streets? What about loading and unloading for shopkeepers? Will that be made impossible?

230376.explanation: And schools should pay more attention to young people who are struggling and tackle bullying.

230320.explanation: A public lobby register promotes transparency. FVD Maastricht supports openness and transparency.

230321.explanation: The municipality of Maastricht spends an enormous amount of money on external agencies. This can be reduced considerably. Tax money must be handled with great care.

230322.explanation: FVD Maastricht prefers direct participation in the form of local referendums on important issues. In practice, we see that the citizen budget often goes to people and organizations with a large network and good connections. As far as we are concerned, that is not the ideal scenario.

230323.explanation: By sending civil servants and officials into the community, we improve, on the one hand, the contact between the municipality and its residents, while on the other hand, civil servants and officials gain a much better understanding of what is happening in the city.

230324.explanation: Democracy is more than just voting once every four years. On important local issues, such as the placement of asylum seeker centers, waste policy, or the introduction of car-free zones, the population of Maastricht must be consulted.

230325.explanation: The municipality currently invests an incredible amount of money in Brightlands. It alone has cost the Maastricht taxpayer €150,000 to change the name of Maastricht Randwyck Station to Maastricht Brightlands Station. This exemplifies the wastefulness regarding this area.

230326.explanation: Property tax (ozb) is one of the most unjust forms of taxation. It must be significantly lowered and absolutely not raised.

230327.explanation: By keeping taxes low, we make Maastricht attractive, and that brings us prosperity.

230328.explanation: Imposing more regulations than necessary is very unwise. Entrepreneurs form the backbone of Maastricht society. They should be facilitated, not burdened with extra regulation.

230329.explanation: Having a pet should not be a cash cow for the municipality of Maastricht. FVD Maastricht is a strong advocate for the abolition of dog tax.

230330.explanation: The money would be better invested in actually making Maastricht more attractive as a location for businesses, rather than just a promotional campaign.

230331.explanation: Entrepreneurs should be free to decide their own opening hours.

230332.explanation: Entrepreneurs should be free to decide their own opening hours.

230333.explanation: It is in no way justifiable that people who have recently arrived here are given priority over born and bred Maastricht residents who have been on a waiting list for years.

230334.explanation: It is up to the international student themselves to integrate into his or her new environment.

230335.explanation: Nothing is free. A 'free language course' in practice means the municipality, and thus the Maastricht taxpayer, pays for it. Nowadays, there are sufficient accessible tools (such as Duolingo) to learn a new language.

230336.explanation: The municipality should no longer accommodate asylum seekers, for the simple reasons that there is no available housing and it leads to friction with the local population.

230337.explanation: Sufficient warnings are given to prevent a bike from ending up at the bicycle depot in the first place.

230338.explanation: The municipality should not unnecessarily obstruct associations.

230339.explanation: The Landbouwbelang has been able to occupy a building free of charge for two decades by squatting. Authorities have been very lenient in this regard. If Landbouwbelang finds and can finance a location themselves, that is fine, but the municipality bears no responsibility here.

230340.explanation: New construction should primarily be realistic and align with existing Maastricht architecture.

230341.explanation: It is unwise to apply stricter policies than necessary.

230342.explanation: FVD Maastricht opposes unnecessary tree felling. Trees should not be cut down arbitrarily. The current rules provide adequately for this.

230343.explanation: Maastricht's reputation is excellent, and it is so attractive that promotion is no longer necessary. Promotion already occurs through residents or tourists recommending Maastricht to others. In addition, Maastricht events generate even more positive attention.

230347.explanation: FVD Maastricht wants more attention for Maastricht culture across all age groups, including young people.

230348.explanation: Large events add extra allure to the city and are good for Maastricht's economy.

230350.explanation: Additional education and homework guidance are responsibilities of parents in collaboration with the school.

230353.explanation: Although FVD Maastricht prefers police deployment, enforcement officers (boa’s) can also contribute to safety. The condition is that they are deployed to enhance safety and not to generate extra income through unnecessary fines.

230354.explanation: FVD Maastricht believes that the uncontrolled number of incorrectly parked bicycles must be tackled.

230355.explanation: It is unnecessary to place cameras everywhere. Moreover, it is an invasion of privacy.

230356.explanation: Completely stopping the growth of student housing is too harsh and infringes on property rights. Nuisance must be addressed where it occurs; student housing should not be banned in advance.

230357.explanation: Taking the position that no new hotels should be allowed in Maastricht at all is too extreme. FVD Maastricht supports tailor-made solutions.

230358.explanation: The loss of parking spaces is not a good development and leads to practical problems. We must be realistic: a car is a necessity for many people in their daily lives.

230360.explanation: It is particularly sad that more and more people cannot find housing in their own community nowadays. The municipality should facilitate this as much as possible.

230361.explanation: Maastricht suffers from a housing shortage. Adding housing units is one of the solutions to the problem.

230362.explanation: What applies to regular homes also applies to pitches for mobile homes. The municipality should relax the rules to meet demand.

230364.explanation: Buying off problematic debts is a very bad idea. It sends the signal that the municipality will ultimately guarantee them. Moreover, it is unfair that the Maastricht taxpayer takes over the debts of debtors.

230365.explanation: People receiving social assistance benefits can certainly contribute to society.

230366.explanation: FVD Maastricht stands for equal treatment for every resident.

230368.explanation: Although FVD Maastricht finds the idea attractive, ultimately this choice should rest with the housing corporation.

230369.explanation: FVD Maastricht advocates sufficient parking spaces throughout Maastricht, including the city center.

230371.explanation: The current cycling routes in the Netherlands and Maastricht are already of excellent quality.

230372.explanation: FVD Maastricht supports sufficient parking spaces throughout Maastricht.

230373.explanation: A good connection with nearby cities is essential.

230374.explanation: The area where parking fees must be paid in Maastricht is already enormous. Even in neighborhoods far from the city center, such as Scharn and Pottenberg, parking fees apply. That is undesirable.

230375.explanation: FVD Maastricht supports a well-accessible city center. This applies to all modes of transport.

230378.explanation: Caregivers are essential to Maastricht society. Support can take many forms, for example by granting a parking permit.

230380.explanation: Nothing is free. In this case, the Maastricht taxpayer foots the bill. Moreover, where do we draw the line? Why provide tampons and sanitary pads, but not toothpaste and toilet paper?

230320.explanation: Governance should be transparent. When senior officials and aldermen meet with lobbyists, Maastricht should know who was at the table and what was discussed. No backroom politics, but honest administration. That is the least residents deserve.

230321.explanation: Calling in an expensive consultancy for every simple question? That is a waste of money. We only hire external experts when truly necessary: for real expertise. The municipality should think for itself, not outsource everything to costly external consultants.

230322.explanation: Residents may provide input, but the municipality must take responsibility itself. Not everything should be pushed onto residents. We see it as our task to actively invest in neighborhoods without people having to lobby for every bench or crosswalk first.

230323.explanation: Policy makers need to know what is really going on. Spending a morning on the work floor says more than a hundred reports. We want civil servants and administrators with their feet on the ground. No ivory towers, but real connection with the city.

230324.explanation: A referendum can be valuable, but it is not enough. Taking residents seriously means continuously listening, explaining, and adjusting. The municipality must go into the neighborhoods and engage in dialogue - not just ask a question but truly collaborate on solutions.

230325.explanation: Investing in Brightlands is investing in Maastricht’s future. It attracts talent and creates jobs at every level, from researchers to support staff. This way, all residents get more opportunities. Smart growth starts with inclusive prosperity.

230326.explanation: Taxing homeowners extra is not fair policy. MAASTRICHT van NU does not want to punish residents for owning a home. We look for other solutions: higher tourist tax, tackling vacancy, and smarter financial management. More revenue without burdening residents.

230327.explanation: Tourists contribute to congestion, so they should also contribute to the solution. MAASTRICHT van NU wants a higher tourist tax so residents can benefit: from clean streets, livable neighborhoods, affordable facilities, and extra efforts to combat poverty.

230328.explanation: Entrepreneurs are not cash machines. MAASTRICHT van NU rejects layered policies where local rules pile on top of national ones. We make entrepreneurship easier, not more complicated. Less paperwork, more opportunities. This keeps Maastricht livable and economically strong.

230329.explanation: Dog tax is outdated and unfair. It punishes animal lovers while other pets are free. MAASTRICHT van NU wants equal treatment and fewer nonsensical charges. Abolish this symbolic tax. It is time for fairer policy.

230330.explanation: Maastricht promotes itself well enough. MAASTRICHT van NU prefers to invest money in what truly attracts businesses: good accessibility, affordable workspaces, and a healthy city. No expensive campaigns, but investing in what works - for entrepreneurs and residents alike.

230331.explanation: Supermarkets may open longer on Sundays, but it must make sense. The extra hours are not a problem as long as both entrepreneur and employee agree. MAASTRICHT van NU chooses tailored solutions: freedom where it works, no pressure where it is unnecessary.

230332.explanation: Sunday openings fit a vibrant city. They benefit tourism, convenience in the neighborhood, and employment. MAASTRICHT van NU opts for modern flexibility, not a nostalgic return to times when Sundays had to be quiet.

230333.explanation: Housing shortage affects all residents. MAASTRICHT van NU believes everyone should have equal chances for social rental housing. No automatic preference, but a fair assessment based on situation, family composition, and urgency. Housing is a right, not a privilege.

230334.explanation: Integration starts with the responsibility of students themselves and the university, not the municipality. MAASTRICHT van NU believes housing pressure and nuisance must be addressed first before investing extra time and money in more cooperation. First balance, then connection.

230335.explanation: Who lives here should be able to participate. Language is the key to work, contact, and neighborhood inclusion. MAASTRICHT van NU wants everyone to have the opportunity to learn Dutch - easily accessible and free. That is not a cost, but an investment in coexistence.

230336.explanation: Maastricht already has enough on its plate. MAASTRICHT van NU believes we should adhere to national agreements. More shelter than required puts extra pressure on housing, facilities, and support. First take care of our own residents.

230337.explanation: €15 is too little, €50 too much. If costs are too low, the nuisance remains; if too high, people leave their bikes at the depot. MAASTRICHT van NU chooses a fair middle ground: €25 is reasonable, encourages collection, and keeps the city orderly. Enforcement with common sense.

230338.explanation: Associations deserve trust, not excessive control. MAASTRICHT van NU wants enforcement focused on real violations, not standard nagging. Encourage self-responsibility and provide space. Rules exist, but enforcement must be with common sense.

230339.explanation: Cultural free spaces belong to a vibrant city. MAASTRICHT van NU supports room for creativity and grassroots initiative - as long as there are clear rules. No uncontrolled growth, no subsidized competition for entrepreneurs. Freedom yes, but fair and responsible.

230340.explanation: Nature is important, but affordable housing comes first. Mandatory nest boxes and green roofs drive up costs while starters and families already struggle to find housing. Encourage where possible, but don’t make it obligatory. You can’t buy a nest box without a roof over your head.

230341.explanation: National rules are sufficient in MAASTRICHT van NU’s view. Imposing extra bans causes confusion and unnecessary pressure on farmers and green managers. Health is important, but policy must also remain feasible and fair. Follow the national line, maintain local calm.

230342.explanation: Trees are valuable, but sometimes space must be made for safety, housing, or livability. MAASTRICHT van NU wants no extra bureaucracy but does want care. Assessment per situation, with attention to greenery and common sense.

230343.explanation: Maastricht promotes itself well enough. MAASTRICHT van NU wants to spend less on glossy campaigns and more on livable neighborhoods and affordable housing. Tourism is welcome, but the city’s interests come first. First invest in residents, then in visitors.

230346.explanation: Geusseltbad is more than a swimming pool. It provides sports, swimming lessons, and cooling - especially for those who cannot go on vacation. It gets too busy in summer. MAASTRICHT van NU wants to explore how expansion can be smart and affordable. Geusseltbad belongs to all of us - so we invest together.

230347.explanation: Culture is not secondary - it gives young people a voice, outlet, and opportunities. MAASTRICHT van NU wants more investment in culture that attracts youth: music, dance, street art, film. Less subsidy for elitist venues, more space for young street talent.

230348.explanation: MAASTRICHT van NU is satisfied with the current approach. We do not need more large events at any cost. Prefer quality over quantity, with attention to livability and support. The city is for residents - not just day trippers.

230349.explanation: Every child must learn to swim - for safety and health. Not all parents can afford it. That is why MAASTRICHT van NU wants school swimming back, supported by the municipality. Swimming is a skill to learn. It is not a luxury, it is a basic right.

230350.explanation: Not every child gets help with school at home, and tutoring is expensive. MAASTRICHT van NU wants free homework support in neighborhoods and retraining for those seeking better jobs. This helps children and parents move forward. A little help can make a big difference.

230353.explanation: Visible enforcement creates safe neighborhoods and less nuisance. MAASTRICHT van NU wants more enforcement officers on the streets, not behind desks. Present in the neighborhood, approachable and with authority. For livability, safety, and trust in public space.

230354.explanation: Incorrectly parked bikes cause chaos and hinder pedestrians, especially elderly and disabled people. MAASTRICHT van NU wants enforcement to be visible, fair, and targeted. No bike dumps on sidewalks. The city belongs to everyone.

230355.explanation: Cameras don’t solve everything, but they help with safety and detection. MAASTRICHT van NU does not want Big Brother, but does want surveillance where needed: in nuisance hotspots, illegal dumping spots, and nightlife areas. Fewer cameras does not mean a better city.

230356.explanation: Residential neighborhoods are for living, not for conversion into student housing. MAASTRICHT van NU wants to restore balance: no wild growth of student homes at the expense of families and community spirit. Students are welcome, but spreading out and clear limits are needed for livable neighborhoods.

230357.explanation: Maastricht has enough hotels. In our view, expansion is unnecessary. MAASTRICHT van NU wants to maintain the balance between tourism and livability. More hotels don’t necessarily add more value to the city but do increase congestion. Setting limits is responsible governance.

230358.explanation: Green is important, but accessibility is too. MAASTRICHT van NU does not want to remove parking spaces if residents are inconvenienced. First provide alternatives and tailor per neighborhood. More green? Gladly. But not by driving out cars without a plan.

230359.explanation: Not every building plan is the same. MAASTRICHT van NU believes you must consider the needs per location. Sometimes affordable home ownership makes sense, sometimes social housing or care homes. One standard does not work - tailored solutions do. You build for what is truly needed.

230360.explanation: Those who grow up here should be able to keep living here. MAASTRICHT van NU wants priority for home seekers from our own city. This keeps neighborhoods lively, associations strong, and families close together.

230361.explanation: MAASTRICHT van NU wants to make room for new homes, including on the edges of the city if necessary. The pressure on the housing market is too great to do nothing. Expansion is possible, provided it is smart, affordable, and mindful of green spaces and livability. Building for the future, not for show.

230362.explanation: Trailer park residents belong here. MAASTRICHT van NU wants them to have equal opportunities for suitable housing. That can only be achieved with more pitches. No discrimination, no delays – just do what is necessary for equal treatment and true freedom of choice.

230364.explanation: MAASTRICHT van NU chooses perspective over procrastination. Problematic debts must be able to be settled under strict conditions. With a plan, mutual commitment, and clear agreements. Not a gift – but a second chance that works.

230365.explanation: Those who can work should contribute back to society. MAASTRICHT van NU believes social assistance is not a dead end. Meaningful work provides rhythm, pride, and perspective. Participation according to ability, with good guidance and attention to what someone can do.

230366.explanation: Participation should not be a luxury. MAASTRICHT van NU wants a social pass for people with low income so they can also play sports, learn, and enjoy culture. Equal opportunities start with access – not the price tag. This is investing in inclusion.

230368.explanation: Anyone who wants and can should be able to buy their rental home. MAASTRICHT van NU wants housing corporations to actively facilitate this. It strengthens ownership, housing turnover, and neighborhood ties. Moreover, it generates extra income for corporations to reinvest in new social housing.

230369.explanation: More parking spaces in the center do not solve anything and actually cause extra congestion, pollution, and less space for pedestrians and cyclists. MAASTRICHT van NU chooses smart mobility: good accessibility but with attention to livability and space in the city.

230370.explanation: Safe traffic starts with speed. MAASTRICHT van NU wants to introduce 30 km/h zones in more residential areas. Especially where there are many children, elderly, and cyclists. Livability and safety take priority over haste.

230371.explanation: Cycling should be safe and logical – also between neighborhoods. MAASTRICHT van NU wants to invest in clear, well-lit cycling routes. No winding paths or unsafe crossings, but real connections where young and old can safely traverse the neighborhood.

230372.explanation: Fewer parking spaces sounds green but often causes more frustration than improvement. MAASTRICHT van NU wants to first provide alternatives, such as parking garages or shared mobility. Not cutting spaces to score points, but plans that really work for the neighborhood.

230373.explanation: A train connection with Hasselt strengthens the region, connects people, and offers sustainable mobility. MAASTRICHT van NU sees this as a smart investment in accessibility, economy, and cross-border cooperation. More connection, less traffic jams – that is progress.

230374.explanation: No extension of paid parking in outer neighborhoods. Tourists cause nuisance, not residents. MAASTRICHT van NU wants higher rates for visitors, not for inhabitants. More support for residents, such as 500 hours of free parking per year. Fair parking without extra burdens.

230375.explanation: Car-free zones can work, but not everything has to be closed off. MAASTRICHT van NU wants tailor-made solutions: space for pedestrians where possible, accessibility where necessary. No blind hatred of cars, but smart choices per street – with residents and entrepreneurs involved.

230376.explanation: Young people are increasingly struggling with mental health issues. MAASTRICHT van NU wants to invest in low-threshold, fast help. No waiting lists, but real support at school, in the neighborhood, and online. Growing up mentally healthy is just as important as learning or sports.

230378.explanation: Informal caregivers are indispensable but often overburdened. MAASTRICHT van NU wants more support: respite care, appreciation, and practical help. Not only when things go wrong, but in time and nearby. Those who care for others deserve care and attention from the municipality too.

230380.explanation: MAASTRICHT van NU recognizes the problem of period poverty but wants targeted help where it is really needed. Not handing out for free everywhere, but ensuring those in need have easy access through schools, support services, or neighborhood points. Practical and focused.

statements.title: The municipality should abolish the dog tax.

titleParts.glossary: Tax paid to the municipality for owning one or more dogs.

statements.moreInfo: In Maastricht residents with a dog must pay dog tax. The municipality can use the proceeds of the dog tax for dog facilities, such as dog parks, and for cleaning up dog waste, but it may also use the revenue for other purposes. In 2026 the dog tax in Maastricht is €77.52 for the first dog.
statements.pro: The dog tax is unfair. There is no tax on other pets. Much of the money goes to administration and enforcement. The dog tax is outdated. In many municipalities there is no longer a dog tax.
statements.con: the dog tax generates revenue for the municipality. The municipality uses this money to cover dog-related costs. Those who cause fouling should pay for it.

statements.title: The fee for collecting a bike from the bike depot should be lowered to €15. It now costs €50.

titleParts.text: De kosten voor het ophalen van een fiets bij het

titleParts.glossary: A place where bicycles that have been removed are stored.

titleParts.text: moeten omlaag naar € 15. Dit kost nu € 50.

statements.moreInfo: The municipality of Maastricht removes bicycles that are broken, have been unused for a long time, or are parked incorrectly. This is done to free up space in bicycle racks and parking facilities. In the center bicycles are also removed if they are parked on the pavement or outside the marked spaces and racks. Removed bicycles are taken to the bicycle depot at Gerardusweg 39. Owners can retrieve their bicycle there. They currently pay €50 to do so.
statements.pro: €50 is too high for retrieving a bicycle. At a lower amount people will pick up their bikes sooner. A lower price fits a city that promotes cycling.
statements.con: A higher amount ensures people comply better with the rules. A low price can lead to more nuisance from incorrectly parked bicycles. The €50 fine covers the municipality's costs.

statements.title: The municipality should offer free language courses to international residents.
statements.moreInfo: Maastricht has many international residents, such as students, expats, and people with a residence permit. The municipality may decide to offer language courses free to international residents. For some groups language lessons are already mandatory. People with a residence permit must take a language course according to the law. For asylum seekers in an asylum center language lessons are not mandatory, but classes are offered. Students can take language courses through their school or university and choose whether to participate. Expats can voluntarily participate in language courses or language initiatives in the city.
statements.pro: Language helps international residents participate in society. With language courses people can find work faster. Learning a language makes contact with others easier.
statements.con: Language courses should be organized nationally, not by the municipality. Free courses are not necessary for everyone. People who can afford it can take a course themselves.

statements.title: The municipality of Maastricht should introduce a public lobby register. A lobby register records who speaks with officials and about what.
statements.moreInfo: Municipal officials, such as aldermen and the mayor, regularly speak with organizations, companies, and interest groups. This is also called lobbying. A public lobbying register records who has spoken with officials and what the conversation was about. Residents can view this register.
statements.pro: A lobby register provides more transparency in government. Residents can see who is trying to exert influence. Transparency increases trust in the municipality.
statements.con: It can be a barrier for people and organizations to contact the municipality. Maintaining a lobby register takes a lot of time and money. A public lobby register goes too far.

statements.title: The municipality should invest more in a promotion campaign for Maastricht as an attractive location for companies.

titleParts.text: De gemeente moet meer geld steken in een

titleParts.glossary: A series of advertisements on, for example, billboards, television, and social media.
The location where a company establishes itself.

statements.moreInfo: Municipalities can attract companies by showing what they have to offer. This can be done, for example, with a promotional campaign. The goal is to convince companies to settle in the municipality.
statements.pro: A promotional campaign helps attract businesses and talent to Maastricht. A promotional campaign can create more jobs. More companies can contribute to the city's economic growth.
statements.con: Maastricht is already attractive enough for businesses. The money would be better spent on accessibility and affordable workspaces. Good conditions for entrepreneurs are more important than advertising.

statements.title: The municipality should try to attract more large events.

titleParts.text: De gemeente moet proberen om meer grote

titleParts.glossary: A temporary, organized gathering. For example a street party, a flea market, or a music festival.

statements.moreInfo: Large events are organized annually in Maastricht, such as fairs, concerts, and sports and cultural events. These events often attract many visitors from both inside and outside the city. The municipality plays an active role, together with external partners, in updating and strengthening the offering of larger events.
statements.pro: Large events attract many visitors to the city. Events provide more income for entrepreneurs. Events make the city livelier and more attractive.
statements.con: Large events can cause nuisance for residents. They put pressure on liveability, such as crowds and noise. The city should focus on quality and public support, not just the number of events.

statements.title: There should be less camera surveillance in the municipality of Maastricht.

titleParts.text: In de gemeente Maastricht moet minder

titleParts.glossary: Monitoring of a building, area, or (groups of) people using security cameras.

statements.moreInfo: The municipality uses camera surveillance to monitor nuisance and crime. Cameras are installed in public spaces and record what happens there. A control room monitors the images to increase safety. In Maastricht cameras are mainly located in the center, Wyck, the entertainment district, and around De Geusselt football stadium. The municipality is expanding camera surveillance to the entire city center and adjacent neighborhoods. In total this involves about 110 fixed cameras. There are also cameras on the access roads to the city center, among other things to check for vehicles that are not allowed because they emit too many harmful substances. At events or in places with a lot of nuisance the municipality can also place temporary cameras.
statements.pro: Camera surveillance comes at the cost of residents' privacy. Cameras do not solve problems but merely shift them. It is unnecessary to place cameras everywhere.
statements.con: Cameras provide a greater sense of security. Cameras are needed in places with a lot of nuisance. Camera surveillance helps in detecting problems.

statements.title: Recognized refugees should not be given priority for social rental housing.

titleParts.text: Statushouders mogen geen voorrang krijgen op

titleParts.glossary: Rental homes that at the start of the tenancy are not more expensive than a certain amount. In 2026 that amount is €932.93 per month.

statements.moreInfo: Status holders are people who have applied for asylum and have been granted a residence permit. They are allowed to stay in the Netherlands. The national government determines every six months how many status holders a municipality must house. That number is based on the municipality’s population. Municipalities may decide who receives priority for a social rental home. Status holders do not automatically receive priority; that depends on the municipality’s rules. In Maastricht about 1,100 social rental homes become available each year. Around 5 to 6 percent of those are allocated with priority to status holders.
statements.pro: Everyone should have the same chance at a social rental home. Because of the priority rule, starters and young people may have to wait longer for housing. Status holders can live temporarily in flexible housing, so priority for social rental homes is not necessary.
statements.con: A permanent home helps status holders to participate in society. According to the law, the municipality is obliged to provide status holders with housing. The housing shortage is not caused by refugees, but by housing policy in recent years.

statements.title: Maastricht should work towards a train connection with Hasselt in Belgium.
statements.moreInfo: Maastricht and Hasselt are close to each other, but there is no train between these cities. Travel is currently possible by bus or by taking a longer train route. A direct train connection could improve cross-border public transport. The municipality can work with other governments to pursue such a connection. In the past there were plans for a tram link between Maastricht and Hasselt. That project was stopped. A legal procedure between Dutch and Belgian authorities about this is still ongoing.
statements.pro: A train connection strengthens cooperation in the region. A train to Hasselt makes it easier to travel onward to cities like Brussels and Antwerp. Traveling by train is sustainable and good for public transport.
statements.con: There is already a good bus connection between Maastricht and Hasselt. A train connection costs a lot of money. The Belgian partners have stopped the tram project. Without certainty about the costs, a new project is not wise.

statements.title: The municipality should allow supermarkets to be open on Sundays until 10:00 PM.
statements.moreInfo: The municipality of Maastricht sets the rules for shop opening hours. These rules serve as frameworks. Shopkeepers are not obliged to open on shopping evenings or shopping Sundays. Supermarkets have different hours than other shops. On Sundays supermarkets in Maastricht are currently allowed to open from 08:00 to 18:00.
statements.pro: Residents get more freedom to choose when to do their shopping. For people who work full time it becomes easier to buy groceries. Entrepreneurs should be able to decide for themselves when their supermarket is open.
statements.con: Sunday should remain a day for rest, family and leisure. Small businesses often cannot keep up with this extension. Extra opening hours increase workload for staff.

statements.title: New construction should from now on be nature-inclusive. That means a home contributes to the development of nature, for example by having built-in nesting boxes.

titleParts.glossary: Building with extra consideration for plants and animals.

titleParts.text: zijn. Dat betekent dat een woning bijdraagt aan de ontwikkeling van de natuur door bijvoorbeeld ingebouwde nestkasten.

statements.moreInfo: The municipality can set requirements for new construction. Nature-inclusive building takes nature and biodiversity into account, for example by providing nesting boxes for birds and bats, green roofs, or space for plants and insects. In Maastricht nature-inclusive building is not yet mandatory. The municipality does provide builders with advice on nature-inclusive measures. Future plans state that Maastricht aims for new construction to routinely take nature into account.
statements.pro: Nature-inclusive building helps plants and animals in the city. Green residential neighborhoods provide a healthier living environment. It makes neighborhoods more resilient to heat and flooding.
statements.con: Nature-inclusive building can make housing more expensive. Additional rules can delay construction projects. New construction should primarily fit with the existing buildings in Maastricht.

statements.title: There should be more parking spaces in the center of Maastricht.
statements.moreInfo: The municipality of Maastricht sets parking policy in the city. Space in the center is limited and is allocated between cars, cyclists, pedestrians, greenery and terraces.
statements.pro: This keeps the city centre easily accessible for visitors and businesses. People who rely on cars get more parking options. New construction projects require additional parking spaces.
statements.con: More parking spaces lead to more traffic and congestion in the city. The city center should have more space for walking and cycling and less for cars. Parking is better located on the outskirts of the city.

statements.title: The municipality should invest more in cultural offerings for young people.
statements.moreInfo: The municipality can provide money for cultural activities for young people, such as music, dance, film and other creative projects. This can be activities organized by young people themselves or programs from cultural institutions. In recent years Maastricht has had special arrangements for youth, such as grants for youth culture and music projects at schools. Cultural institutions that receive municipal funding also run activities for young people.
statements.pro: Culture helps young people develop their talents. Cultural activities give young people a place to meet each other. There is too little cultural programming that suits young people.
statements.con: The municipality already spends a lot on culture. Additional funding is not needed. There is already enough cultural provision for young people. The money would be better used for sports and places where young people can meet.

statements.title: The municipality should expand the city to be able to build more homes.
statements.moreInfo: The municipality can build new homes on sites within the city, such as vacant lots or former industrial sites. The municipality can also build outside the city; this is called expanding the city. In Maastricht the plan is to build new homes within the city first, for example in larger development areas and in existing neighborhoods, especially close to amenities. If building within the city is not sufficient, the municipality can consider expansion on the city’s edge. The municipality decides where and how this happens.
statements.pro: This can create more housing. It can help address the housing shortage. Affordable homes for young people can be built.
statements.con: There is still space to build within the city. Building outside the city comes at the expense of green areas. Expanding the city can later lead to vacancy.

statements.title: A 30 km/h speed limit should apply in more places within residential neighborhoods.

titleParts.text: Op meer plekken in woonwijken moet een

titleParts.glossary: The maximum speed that cars and motorcycles are allowed to drive on the road.

statements.moreInfo: The built-up area is the part of the city or village where people live, work and shop. A maximum speed of 50 km/h often applies there now, but some streets are already 30 km/h.
statements.pro: 30 km/h makes residential neighbourhoods safer. Slower driving is better for children and the elderly. 30 km/h makes streets quieter and nicer to live on.
statements.con: Additional 30 km/h zones cause frustration and delays. Through roads are made for 50 km/h and are often already safe enough. It is better to enforce only on streets where there are real problems.

statements.title: The municipality should invest more in Brightlands Maastricht Health Campus.
statements.moreInfo: Brightlands Maastricht Health Campus is a location in the Randwyck district where work is done on health, care and innovation. It houses companies, researchers and educational institutions.
statements.pro: More funding for Brightlands creates more jobs in Maastricht. The campus attracts talent and companies to the city. Investments can drive economic growth for the whole city.
statements.con: The municipality has already spent enough on Brightlands. Extra money only makes sense if it clearly yields results. Municipal funds would be better used for other plans in the city.

statements.title: The municipality should host more asylum seekers than the nationally required number.

titleParts.text: De gemeente moet meer dan het landelijk verplichte aantal

titleParts.glossary: People who have applied for asylum and are still waiting for a decision.

statements.moreInfo: People who flee can apply for asylum. The Immigration and Naturalisation Service (IND) decides whether they may remain in the Netherlands. During this procedure asylum seekers must be accommodated. According to national agreements Maastricht must accommodate at least 493 asylum seekers. The municipality is currently accommodating more people than required. In total there are over 800 places, including a permanent asylum seekers’ centre (azc) and temporary emergency accommodation.
statements.pro: A large city can accommodate more asylum seekers than a small village. Additional reception capacity helps reduce the shortage of places in the Netherlands. We must provide a safe place for people fleeing war.
statements.con: Maastricht must adhere to the number agreed at the national level. More reception places put additional pressure on housing. The city already has a large asylum seekers’ centre and already accommodates many people.

statements.title: The municipality should more strictly enforce rules against improperly parked bicycles.
statements.moreInfo: Sometimes bicycles are parked where they are not allowed, for example on sidewalks or at entrances. This can cause obstruction or unsafe situations. The municipality can check and remove bicycles or issue fines. The municipality removes bikes that are broken or appear unused for a long time. A broken bike is called a bicycle wreck. A bike that has stood in the same place for a long time is called an abandoned bike. Bikes that are parked incorrectly, for example outside marked bays or on the sidewalk, can also be removed. Extra checks are carried out in the center and around the station. Removed bikes are kept at the bicycle depot on Gerardusweg. Bicycle wrecks are not stored. The municipality decides how strict its enforcement is.
statements.pro: Improperly parked bicycles cause problems for pedestrians. Elderly people and those with disabilities are especially affected. Stricter enforcement is part of a safe and accessible cycling city.
statements.con: There are too few bicycle parking facilities in the city. Stricter enforcement punishes cyclists for a lack of spaces. Awareness and better parking work better than fines.

statements.title: The property tax (ozb) should be increased.

titleParts.glossary: Tax you pay as the owner of a building or piece of land, or as the user of a commercial property.

statements.moreInfo: The property tax (onroerendezaakbelasting, ozb) is a tax people pay if they own a house or another building. How much you pay depends on the value of your house or building and the rates set by the municipality. The revenue from the property tax is used by the municipality for things such as roads, green spaces and support for residents.
statements.pro: People with a more expensive house can pay more tax than those with a cheaper house. The property tax (ozb) in Maastricht is lower than in comparable municipalities and could therefore be raised slightly. A higher ozb can be used to build new and affordable housing.
statements.con: A higher property tax (OZB) leads to higher housing costs for residents. The OZB has already risen in recent years. The municipality should first cut spending.

statements.title: The municipality should ask the residents of Maastricht for their opinion on important issues via a referendum.

titleParts.text: De gemeente moet de inwoners van Maastricht bij belangrijke onderwerpen om hun mening vragen via een

titleParts.glossary: A public consultation where residents can express their opinion on a political issue. The municipal council is not obliged to follow the result; legally it cannot.

statements.moreInfo: The municipality of Maastricht has established a referendum ordinance. It states that the municipal council can decide to hold a referendum. A referendum is a way for residents to express their opinion on an important topic. A referendum can take place only if a majority of the municipal council agrees on a specific subject. The result of a referendum in the Netherlands is not binding, which means the municipal council can make a different decision.
statements.pro: A referendum is a good way to ask residents for their opinion. On important issues, residents should have the final say. A referendum ensures the municipal government listens better to residents.
statements.con: A referendum only gives a yes-or-no answer and leaves little room for nuance. For complicated issues, consultation and participation work better than a referendum. Referendums cost a lot of time and money and deliver little.

statements.title: Maastricht should give housing applicants from the municipality as much priority as possible when allocating social rental housing.

titleParts.text: Maastricht moet woningzoekenden uit de eigen gemeente zoveel mogelijk voorrang geven bij de toewijzing van een

titleParts.glossary: A rental home with a low rent (in 2026 at most €932.93 per month) for people with a low income.

statements.moreInfo: Municipalities may set their own rules for allocating social rental housing. These rules are in a housing allocation ordinance. The municipality can determine which housing applicants get priority. Some groups can be given priority, such as informal caregivers or people temporarily living in shelters. Since 2017 people with a residence permit do not automatically get priority. Municipalities may decide whether to give this group priority. Municipalities may also designate other groups, for example people with a strong connection to the municipality. In Maastricht at least 45 percent of social rental homes that become available are currently allocated to housing applicants from Maastricht.
statements.pro: Residents who have been on the waiting list for a long time should be given priority. People who grew up in Maastricht should be able to keep living here. Local priority creates strong neighbourhoods and social networks.
statements.con: People from outside Maastricht should also have an equal chance at housing. You solve housing shortages by building more, not by changing priority rules. Social rental housing is for everyone who lives here and participates, not just for Maastricht residents.

statements.title: The municipality should introduce a social pass that allows low-income people to participate in sports, culture and courses for free or at a discount.
statements.moreInfo: The municipality can help residents with low incomes participate in sports, culture and courses. This can be done with a social pass. With such a pass people can take part in activities for free or at a discount, such as sports, going to the theater or following a course. Maastricht already has schemes that allow residents to participate for free or at low cost, such as the Youth Fund for Sport and Culture and the Adult Fund for Sport and Culture. The municipality can decide to extend this help with a social pass.
statements.pro: With a social pass, people on low incomes can take part in sports, cultural activities, and courses. Participation should not depend on money. Everyone should have the same opportunities. The social pass lowers barriers and makes participation easier.
statements.con: There are already schemes that allow low-income people to participate. One more scheme would make the system more confusing. A social pass was tried before and did not work well.

statements.title: Every building plan should include homes for sale priced up to €280,000.
statements.moreInfo: For new construction the municipality makes agreements with project developers about the types of homes to be built. This can include rental homes, owner-occupied homes or a mix. The municipality can set requirements on the price of owner-occupied homes, for example by setting a maximum price. Owner-occupied homes up to €280,000 are intended to be relatively affordable. In Maastricht it currently applies that for building plans of 10 or more homes at least 60 percent must be affordable. This means social rental homes (with rent up to a maximum of €932.93 per month) or affordable owner-occupied homes (with a price up to a maximum of €480,000).
statements.pro: Affordable owner-occupied homes allow more people to buy a house. First-time buyers and young families thus get more opportunities in the housing market. Housing should remain affordable for people with a normal income.
statements.con: A fixed requirement in every building plan can result in fewer homes being built. Not every location is suitable for the same type of housing; tailor-made solutions per site are needed. Fixed rules can actually slow down movement in the housing market.

statements.title: The municipality should more strictly enforce alcohol rules at local associations.
statements.moreInfo: Strict rules apply to serving alcohol. These rules are in national laws and municipal regulations. They also apply to associations, such as sports clubs. For example, associations may not serve alcohol to young people under 18. The municipality checks whether associations comply with these rules. In Maastricht BOAs (municipal enforcement officers) do this. They carry out checks, especially on busy days and at events. Extra attention is paid to alcohol use by minors. Events organized by associations are always checked. Regular club nights are usually checked only if complaints are received, for example from neighbors.
statements.pro: The rules exist to protect young people. Those rules must be followed. Strict enforcement helps prevent alcohol consumption under the age of 18. Enforcement is needed for dangerous behaviour, such as alcohol poisoning.
statements.con: Associations run on volunteers and should not be burdened further. The municipality should support and trust associations, not punish them. Enforcement should only occur in cases of real problems or violations.

statements.title: People receiving social assistance should, in return, do socially useful work.

titleParts.glossary: Money from the municipality for people who have no income or personal funds.
For example cleaning the streets or keeping company and supporting elderly residents.

statements.moreInfo: People receiving social assistance benefits get money from the municipality because they have no or insufficient income. They often also have obligations, such as looking for work or receiving guidance. Some municipalities have people on social assistance do socially useful work. This is work that benefits the community, such as helping in a community center, green maintenance or supporting activities. It is usually unpaid work. The municipality can choose to make this type of work compulsory for people on social assistance.
statements.pro: Those who can work should give something back to society. Community work provides structure and routine. Meaningful work helps people move toward paid employment.
statements.con: Not everyone on welfare can do community work. Mandatory unpaid work is undesirable. Voluntary participation with guidance works better than coercion.

statements.title: The municipality should allocate more money to the participatory budget. With this budget, Maastricht residents decide together how part of the municipal funds are spent.
statements.moreInfo: The municipality of Maastricht prepares an annual budget. The budget shows how much money the municipality receives and what that money is spent on. With a participatory budget residents decide themselves about part of the municipal money. The municipality makes an amount available and residents choose together what that money is spent on. Maastricht already has a participatory budget. Residents can submit proposals and vote together on which ideas are implemented. In 2025 a total of €750,000 was available for the participatory budget.
statements.pro: Residents know best what is needed in their neighbourhood or city. A participatory budget gives residents real influence over municipal funds. Deciding together about money builds more trust.
statements.con: The current budget for the citizens’ budget is already high enough. The citizens’ budget mainly reaches people with time and a large network. Additional money would be better spent directly on neighbourhoods and associations.

statements.title: The municipality should halt the increase in the number of student houses in residential neighbourhoods.
statements.moreInfo: Many students live in Maastricht. Some students live together in ordinary residential neighborhoods, in so-called student houses. Maastricht’s housing policy includes rules for student housing. These rules cover, for example, splitting houses into separate rooms.
statements.pro: Too much student housing causes nuisance in residential neighbourhoods. Neighbourhoods must remain livable for residents. Students are better off living on campuses than in residential areas.
statements.con: A complete stop is too strict and unnecessary. Students are also residents of the city and belong here. A good mix of housing in the city is better than a hard separation.

statements.title: The municipality should ensure there remains room for independent cultural spaces in the city, such as the Landbouwbelang.

titleParts.text: De gemeente moet ervoor zorgen dat er ruimte blijft voor

titleParts.glossary: Places where artists and makers can work and experiment freely.

titleParts.text: in de stad, zoals het Landbouwbelang.

statements.moreInfo: Cultural free spaces are places where people make culture together, such as music and art. This often happens voluntarily and non-commercially. These places provide room for new ideas and for people who cannot find space elsewhere. The municipality can help by providing space or adjusting rules. The municipality decides if and how it supports these places.
statements.pro: Cultural free spaces contribute to a lively city. They provide room for creativity and new ideas. They are important for encounters between different people.
statements.con: The municipality is not responsible for paying for free cultural spaces. Free spaces should not create unfair competition for entrepreneurs. The same rules should apply to everyone, including cultural free spaces.

statements.title: The municipality should encourage housing corporations to give social tenants the opportunity to buy their homes.

titleParts.glossary: Non-profit organizations focused on building, managing and renting affordable housing.

titleParts.text: aanmoedigen om sociale huurders de mogelijkheid te geven om hun woning te kopen.

statements.moreInfo: Social housing is a form of rental housing for people with low incomes. Some social tenants want to buy their rented home. In the Netherlands this is often possible under the Promotion of Home Ownership Act. This means a tenant can buy the home if the landlord (for example a housing association) allows it.
statements.pro: Homeownership gives residents more security. It increases involvement in the neighbourhood. The proceeds can be used for new social rental housing.
statements.con: The stock of social rental housing is already too small. Selling social rental homes lengthens the waiting lists. Social rental housing is intended for people who cannot buy.

statements.title: The municipality should distribute free tampons and sanitary pads in more places.
statements.moreInfo: Menstrual products are basic necessities that women and girls need during their periods. Not everyone can always afford or easily obtain these products. In the municipality of Maastricht there are already distribution points for menstrual products, such as tampons and sanitary pads. These points are currently managed by the Poverty Fund, an organization that helps people with little money. The municipality can choose to distribute free tampons and pads in more locations, for example at public buildings, sports facilities or schools.
statements.pro: Menstrual products are basic necessities and should be available to everyone. Not everyone can easily afford tampons and sanitary pads. That way girls and women do not have to stay home because of their period.
statements.con: Free menstrual products are already available in various places. Providing these products is not the municipality’s task. Free products create additional costs for the municipality.

The municipality should invest more money in mental health care for young people.
The tourist tax should be increased.
The municipality should spend less money promoting tourism to Maastricht.
There should be more pitches for residential caravans.
No new hotels should be allowed in Maastricht.
The municipality should allow fewer shopping Sundays. Currently shops in Maastricht are open every Sunday.
The municipality should invest more money in safe bicycle routes between neighborhoods.
The municipality should allocate money for school swimming.
The municipality should invest more money in additional training and homework tutoring.
More car-free streets should be created in Maastricht's city center.
The municipality should pay off residents' problem debts under strict conditions.
Residential neighborhoods should have fewer parking spaces to make room for more green space.
The municipality should reduce the number of on‑street parking spaces.
Stricter rules should apply to cutting down trees.

The municipality should require civil servants and officials to regularly undertake work placements in the community during working hours.
